### Huck — 2017-07-21T16:03:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-219616>

Last fall I had to give up bacon and greatly reduce other meat consumption to keep my BP from spiking to extremely high (220/130) levels. For the last 10 months, I have been eating a high starch diet (mainly potatoes for starch, fruits, and vegetables), low fat diet. Coconut oil, ghee, and butter all caused BP spikes, so I used mainly olive oil and a little sesame oil. I ate quite a bit of flax, so picked up some fats there. Also took fish oil daily.

I did eat some fish and seafood, chicken/turkey, and small amounts of grass fed beef, but limited to a few times a week.

I initially lost about 35 lbs and have stayed within 7 lbs (up and down) since then. (That is total of 85 lbs from high).

My gut tells me I can eat bacon again, so I had 2 slices with breakfast this morning. In the past, bacon in the morning seemed to control hunger better than anything else, so I hope I can add it back in. I'm hoping that I can add more saturated fats in, at least for the next month or 2, and can lose another 20 lbs.

It looks like my seasons are flipped - fats in summer and carbs in winter.

Just watched this video this morning. I think it helped me decide what I'm going to do after I sell my house.

Video is of a couple who decided to give up their home and job and live in an Airstream trailer full-time, primarily on Bureau of Land Management (BLM) dispersed camping sites (free). If you don't watch the entire video, the key part that relates to us starts around 37:40. You can also set speed to 1.25.

### Huck — 2017-07-26T01:29:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-220082>

After just 3 days of eating 2 slices of bacon with breakfast, bp went through the roof. When I got up this morning it was 164/110 and stayed high for about 3 hours. I drank a large glass of water but didn't eat anything. Went outside and walked 3/4 mile barefoot and then worked in garden for a while. Mowed part of the lawn with push mower.

By noon, BP had dropped to 114/72. Ate 2 baked potatoes (1 for lunch, 1 for dinner). At 6 pm, BP is 113/71.

I still don't understand what about saturated fat triggers the BP spike. At least I know that fasting and potatoes bring BP back down, but it still is scary when BP spikes. Usually heart rate also jumps way up, I start feeling hot, flushed, get a headache, start burping, and have heartburn.

I think part of what caused the sudden spike was that I had just started going out and walking barefoot early mornings which had stabilized my BP at a really good place. I started feeling real bad - no energy, fatigue, just wanted to sleep all day - and didn't go out for my morning walks. So I'm guessing I had a hormonal relapse at the same time I increased saturated fat and that resulted in the sudden BP spike.

### Huck — 2017-08-03T14:51:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-220812>

Another night in ER.

About 10 days ago, BP changed for the worse. It started out high in morning and stayed high until late afternoon. Then at night it would go back up again. Every day the peak got a little higher. (This is with med that has worked for last 6 months).

As in the past, my left arm (shoulder) would start hurting a little, I would get heartburn, and my face would feel hot and flushed. That didn't send me to ER, but when I finally fell asleep last night, I had a real short dream about death. First time I ever remember dreaming about death. About 2 hours later I was able to fall back to sleep and had another dream about death, different from the 1st one.

I couldn't get back to sleep, so after about an hour I checked BP. It was pretty high. I took meds and rechecked an hour later. BP was even higher. I decided to go to ER because I don't know any doctor that will do anything other than prescribe more meds. I knew that's what ER would do as well, but I needed an EKG and a blood test to be sure I wasn't having a heart attack.

EKG and blood test were fine, so after about 2 1/2 hrs in ER, my BP was low enough to be released (about 155/90). The ER doc said that normally they refer blood pressure cases to cardiologists. I asked if there was a liver specialist here and he said no, I would probably have to go to a university to find one.

I have been getting morning sun and grounding, getting afternoon sun and grounding, and working outside most of the day. A low saturated fat diet was the one thing I could count on to keep bp under control, and it doesn't help anymore. I do use olive oil, flax, and fish oil as well as seafood for fats. I either work outside or walk just about every day. I have lost a considerable amount of weight. I get lots of sun. I take l-arginine, vitamin c, vitamin d (sun won't raise d high enough on its own), and other supplements that help lower bp.

I am at a complete loss as to what to try next.

Saw a new cardiologist today. The worse doctor's appointment I ever had, and that's saying a lot.

I am scheduled for a stress test and echocardiogram next week which I will do, but will see Nurse Practitioner for results rather than the doctor. At that point I will get a copy of my records never to return.

I have seen 3 local cardiologists and each one is worse than the previous. The thing is, I have a slight heart murmer but no other heart problems have been found. I just want them to figure out what causes the blood pressure spikes, but they act like they don't hear me. All they want to do is prescribe another drug.

### Huck — 2017-08-17T23:57:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-221839>

Still haven't figured out what is causing recent spikes. Had one this morning when I was at doctor's for cardiac stress test. This one was a little different in that diastolic and heart rate were very high and systolic was only moderately high. It did make it easier to get my heart rate to 130 on the treadmill. (normally it is about 70 and it was 100).

If I wasn't having the spike, I don't think I could have got it that high. I had to wait for about 30 minutes before the final heart monitoring and rechecked heart rate. It was down from 100 to about 72.

I rechecked again when I got to my car. It was still high, but substantially lower than when I got there. Rechecked when I got home and it was back under 120/80 and heart rate was in 60's.

I stopped all dairy about 3 days ago. Nasal congestion that I have had all my adult life is nearly gone. I felt good 2 days in a row and energy was better than normal.

I'm thinking about becoming a pescetarian! I occasionally eat meat, but I think I might feel better if I dropped meat and replaced it with fish and seafood.

### Huck — 2017-08-22T00:41:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222017>

I told my chiropractor that I thought my BP spikes might be related to my liver. He recommended a liver cleanse (I did one a couple of months ago) and he said to take 5 capsules 3 times a day instead of 3 capsules as recommended on container. He also recommended a couple other supplements.

I started waking at 2 am and couldn't get back to sleep. After the 2nd night that it happened, I looked up waking at 2 am and found some articles that said Chinese medicine said that was when the liver was most active. I stopped the supplements and haven't had the problem again. I restarted the cleanse supplements, but at a much lower dosage.

I have had good energy and a positive attitude for a week straight. Ever since I stopped all dairy, I have felt like what I think a normal person feels like! I can do physical work for hours without getting tired or frustrated. I'm even being more social.

### Huck — 2017-08-23T20:49:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222103>

I must have white coat hypertension (in addition to the spikes).

I had a cardiac stress test last week and my BP was very high when I got there. It got to over 200/110 during stress test. An hour after getting home, BP was back below 120/80. This morning I had an appointment just to go over stress test and echo. BP was about 170/95 (nurse). I tested immediately after appt as soon as I got to my car - 160/100. I tested 40 minutes later (same BP meter) when I got home and BP was under 120/80.

So even though I have BP spikes, their answer was come back in 6 months.

>
> Never in a million years would I have put this together without Doc. I'd have been playing niacin games. The man bought me more time with my potential future grandbabies. Priceless.

That's a possibility I hadn't thought of. It happens in every doctor's office where my BP has been checked. It very well might be EMF plus a little stress. What's amazing to me is how quickly it goes up when I get there and how quickly it comes down when I leave.

The nurse practitioner I saw today actually told me that they know that sitting in the waiting room, being called by the nurse, having your weight and temperature taken, and then having BP taken after just sitting for a minute or 2, that BP is going to test high.

### Huck — 2017-08-24T17:10:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222176>

Got results of echocardiogram and stress test yesterday. Stress test was OK.

The echo showed that 2 years ago I had **mild** aortic stenosis and now it is **moderate**.

The info I found (Dr Davis) on the web recommends getting vitamin d over 70 and taking 1000 mcg/day of K2 which includes both MK-4 and MK-7, to slow, stop, or possibly repair aortic stenosis. My vitamin D should be OK and I just restarted vitamin K2 a week ago. I ordered LEF K2 which meets that recommendation.

The LEF site has an article that says there is an association between high ldl, triglycerides, and total cholesterol, and a higher rate of aortic stenosis. I plan on retesting lipids in a month or 2 to see if change in diet has brought ldl and triglycerides down.

It appears from the echo I have a bicuspid aortic valve (2 flaps instead of 3). A little over 1% of adults have it. The nurse practitioner and I were brainstorming about what might be causing my BP spikes, and she wondered if it could be related to my bicuspid valve.

I'd be interested in any info others have on aortic stenosis and what I can do to prevent it getting worse.

It is suggested that from that time on there will also be a clinical benefit for the subject, but these processes proceed slowly and a measurable benefit in a clinical trial (vitamin K2 over placebo) becomes visible after at least one year, with a more pronounced effect after 2 and 3 years.

Where did your J progenitors types get their bio-avalible K2 from? Amazon.com?
>
> It's your journal and I'll shut my piehole now.

To get enough MK-7 to reverse aortic stenosis, I think one almost has to take k2 supplements. You can get enough K1 and the MK-4 type of K2 from food, but I don't see how I can get 100 mcg of MK-7 from food. My progenitors likely had the right biome and ate the right foods to not have a calcified heart valve in the first place. I need to get a lot of MK-7 into my body quickly or face the real possibility of needing heart surgery in my 70's or 80's.

I've tried CT baths for several weeks a few of years ago with no noticeable improvements. I found them a real challenge and after several weeks I still found them very uncomfortable. Now, I do spot icing (mainly on stomach) and have an ice vest that I use.

I spend about half of the year in a pair of shorts with no shirt. I built a privacy fence around my patio so I can nude sunbathe.

Without vitamin d supplementation, I couldn't get my vitamin d levels above the 20's. What I found was that by getting d higher through supplementation, sun exposure drove it even higher.

I agree that most supplements are a total waste of money, but every once in a while one works wonders. (Listen to Jack's webinar on prostate cancer where he recommends several supplements). When I learned my aortic stenosis had gotten worse, this was the 1st place I looked for advice. Jack had responded to someone who asked about aortic stenosis - look up K2 and Framingham study - people with highest levels of K2 had lowest incidence of heart disease. From that I found info where Dr Davis was having success using high doses of K2 supplementation in stopping and sometimes reversing stenosis.

I watched a video yesterday where a patient reversed his high blood pressure with high doses of vitamin d and vitamin k supplements. After several weeks he was able to get off all his bp meds and his bp was significantly lower than when on medications. I mention this because after just a couple of days of taking a high dose of K2, my BP was around 110/70 all day yesterday. This gives me hope that k2 may reverse my valve calcification.

drezy, I really appreciate your insight. The reason I asked the question the other day about genetics vs epigentics is because people here discuss things like MTHFR and supplements to take for it. Why? Can't it be overridden by epigenetics?

I have a congenital aortic valve defect. According to conventional medicine, the only treatment is surgery. Can epigenetics fix it?

I have snp's that indicate trouble digesting and metabolizing fat. What's the epigenetic solution? I have another snp that indicates difficulty making vitamin d from sunlight. What is the epigenetic solution?

Why is the MTHFR genetic mutation considered differently than other snp's?

ETA: After I posted I realized this might come across as argumentative. I was actually trying to explain my logic on why I am doing what I am doing. My n=1 seems to be a lot different from most. About a year ago, I realized that following the path taken by others wasn't getting me the results they were getting. I started going with what worked for me, even when it was the opposite of what Jack or Paleo or some other plan said was best.

Since then I have lost about 40 lbs (90 total) and got rid of cravings. My every day bp is getting better all the time, even though I haven't figured out the cause of the spikes yet.

I spent over 15 years on a low carb, high fat diet losing the same 50 lbs over and over again. The epi-paleo diet resulted in the same thing. It wasn't until I went low fat that the weight came off and stayed off.

Do I want to be able to eat steaks and butter again? You better believe it, but my body can't handle that much fat.

### drezy — 2017-08-25T21:00:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222245>

I'm glad to be wrong about your solar exposure and I apologize for missing it back in your thread. That's on me.

I'm not trashing supplements per se, but I do look at anything that I think about taking from the perspective of how did my ancestors get it. There is always the stark possibility that they just plain died earlier. It's a harsh possibility, but worth considering for a fuller picture.

I'm glad that you experienced some health gains with this method. Thank you for educating me Huck:

From [https://www.jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/](https://www.jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/)

> For every increase of **10 micrograms** in the amount of vitamin K2 consumed daily, the risk of developing coronary heart disease (CHD) drops by **9 percent**. This statistic was noted as a result of a cohort study from the Netherlands evaluating the dietary vitamin K intakes of 16,057 post-menopausal women and their association with the incidence of CHD. Many people/doctors seem unaware of this data. When I see a low Vitamin D levels I usually tell the person with bad bones, a bad heart or bad teeth to increase their vitamin K2 200mcgs for every 1000 IU of D 3 they are supplementing. This is the magic that helps them “begin” to rehydrate.
>
>
> -   [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19179058](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19179058)
> -   [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11180916](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11180916)

I'll share my undeveloped take on epigenetic and SNPS I've gathered here so far

Who adapts faster to environments between prokaryotes and eukaryotes? Prokaryotes
How do they do this? horizontal gene transfer (genetic thievery of sorts)
But which (pro vs eukaryotes) are you and I? Both, They "use" us for more effective membrane area and we "use" them for a lot more

Odd side note: Only females transfer their mitochondria so as a male, from a mito perspective, you are literally just being used for sex and kicked out. Imagine that.

Where was this symbiotic relationship developed in humans? Equatorial Africa with it's massive amount of UV

The mega-membranes (our ancestors) decided to migrate at some point to places with different solar output changed quite radically when you consider Non-linear effects.

\*This much heavy thinking always requires me to have Bollywood-like dance break in the middle. Here Gloria will describe maybe how those little mitos felt about being moved to from such environments:

\[Note changes in red and please sing along for best fact retention. Dancing is optional but recommended. I tried to edit the least of Gloria's words out of respect\]

_**At first I was afraid, I was petrified**_
_**Kept thinking I could never live without UV inside**_
_**But then I spent so many nights thinking how you did me wrong**_
_**And I grew strong**_
_**And I learned how to get along**_
_**And so you're back**_
_**From outer space**_
_**I just walked in to find that you’d become a migratory race**_
_**I should have changed your bio clock, I should have made you be a tree**_
_**If I'd known for just one second you'd be relocating me**_
_**Go on now, go, walk out the door**_
_**Just turn around now**_
_**'Cause you're not coupled anymore**_
_**Weren't you the one who tried to move from Olduvai**_
_**Do you think I'd crumble**_
_**Did you think I'd lay down and die?**_

_**Oh no, not I, I will survive**_
_**Oh, as long as I know how to love, I know I'll stay alive**_
_**I've got all my life to live**_
_**And I've got ATP to give and I'll survive**_
_**I will survive, hey, hey**_

_**It took all the strength I had not to fall apart**_
_**Kept trying hard to mend the pieces of my broken heart**_
_**And I spent oh-so many nights just feeling sorry for myself**_
_**I used to cry**_
_**But now I hold my head up high and you see me**_
_**Somebody new**_
_**I'm not that chained-up little cell when I can edit you**_
_**And so you felt like dropping in and just expecting ATP**_
_**Well, now I'm making little changes deep inside of your COMT**_

_**Go on now, go, walk out the door**_
_**Just turn around now**_
_**'Cause you're not coulped anymore**_
_**Weren't you the one who tried to move from Olduvai**_
_**Do you think I'd crumble**_
_**Did you think I'd lay down and die?**_
_**Oh no, not I, I will survive**_
_**.**_
_**.**_

That's probably not as useful to you on second thought. I just had a personal stretch goal of making @[Huck](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1100/) sing and dance.

epigenetic = "above" genetics

From the Viral Marketing blog

> The difference was that the leaky gut was an adaptation that sculpted us from primates, by using rapid gene transfer from our gut flora to our own genome to cause a massive genetic assimilation of these retroviruses, while the food source became very nutrient dense simultaneously.

From [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2945002/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2945002/)

> One example is loci that have biologically predictable functions but genotypes that fail to correlate with phenotype, particularly disease outcome. Conversely, a crosstalk between genetics and epigenetics may provide a mechanistic explanation for cases in which a convincing association between phenotype and a genetic variant has been established, but the latter does not lie in a promoter or protein coding sequence.

See hanging around with Doc gives you good questions. The researchers above might be able to beat you if this were a scrabble game, but you're barking up the same tree as them.

I currently understand congenital as being stuck there aka even as cells naturally experience autophagy the new cells take on the old physical pattern for an individual. I suspect that my view will be proven wrong soon though in part because of in vivo CRISPR experiments:
[http://www.nature.com/nbt/journal/v33/n4/full/nbt.3155.html?foxtrotcallback=true](http://www.nature.com/nbt/journal/v33/n4/full/nbt.3155.html?foxtrotcallback=true)
I've noticed a trend that \*every\* trick we "discover" is already in play in our nature. Nature seems to have played every trick in the book and then written 4 sequels. We're getting access to sloppy (scientific) seconds with discoveries.

I'll share my snps understanding next

### drezy — 2017-08-25T21:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222247>

Tech geekery:
I like what JMO had here:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...nps-effect-redox-potential.17411/#post-186867](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-do-mthfr-snps-effect-redox-potential.17411/#post-186867)

Since you've spent time in IT I've been trying to pack this into a datacenter analogy in my head. All analogies have faults though.

I imagine a datacenter and zoom in on a sub cluster of a few different types of systems (x86, sparcs, and risc machines).
Each sub-cluster is differentiated for potentially separate functions (like cells) and has monitoring systems (epigenetics/mitochondria).
All systems running is 100% ideal usage.
A series of jobs(environmental change) may come in that is so ill suited to the sparc machines that it's best to sleep or power them down (SNPS)
There may be ways to alter some of the jobs to execute them also on the RISC machines (more SNPS)

> people here discuss things like MTHFR and supplements to take for it. Why? Can't it be overridden by epigenetics?

I can only speak for 667+/-, but I can override much if not all of deleterious effects related to it. I don't view it as a "defect" anymore though. I think it's an alteration in place to optimize life in some environment other than equatorial Africa. If I'm mindful of my quantum yield and I hit the cold tub (aka datacenter chiller boost) my HR & BP are better than 15 years ago and my strength and stamina are on par with about 20 years ago.

Everyone's mileage will vary, but the cold tub was a big win for me. You are a Northern Euro haplo. In theory it should be very useful to you. I think DHA increases the ability to utilize cold. Hmm, Jan and I on the same side of maybe more measuring is like an eclipse.

### Huck — 2017-08-27T14:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222350>

I'm about halfway through Dr Gundy's new book plus listened to some of his interviews. The one he did with Dave Asprey is excellent. It is just 2 brilliant guys talking about health and letting us listen in. At the end, in response to a prompt from Dave, Dr Gundry lists the 3 most important things a person can do to improve health:

1.  Avoid lectins
2.  Intermittent Fasting
3.  Avoid blue light at night

The diet Dr Gundry recommends is one I have been moving to intuitively since last year - vegetables, fish, seafood. The missing piece was lectins. I plan on doing my grocery shopping Monday and giving his plan a try.

Two of the benefits of his diet are normal blood pressure and healthy heart. I think removing lectins and supplementing with vitamin k2 should reverse my heart valve problem.

I have theorized that my BP spikes were caused by eating saturated fat and having undigested fat and bacteria leak into the bloodstream due to poor fat digestion, and that the cure was a low fat diet. ( I have explained this to several MDs, who just shake their head and give me their doctor look. Then they say "You're under-medicated.)

My problem was that low fat worked most of the time, but not all of the time. My daily BP was fine, but I still have dangerously high, unexplained BP spikes. I was close.

A couple of short quotes from Dr Gundry's book ([The Plant Paradox](https://www.amazon.com/Plant-Paradox-Dangers-Healthy-Disease-ebook/dp/B01IMYGX8S/ref=dp_kinw_strp_1)) which explain more scientifically what I was theorizing:

> LPS are fragments of bacteria that are constantly being produced as bacteria divide and die in your gut. They travel through your gut wall and out into your body by riding on and hiding in saturated fat. p 64

And later discussing a low fat diet:

> Because they have eliminated fats, low fat diets do not allow LPSs, which have to travel on long-chain saturated fatty acids to sneak through the gut wall, where they would cause inflammation. p 155-155

Dr Gundry confirmed exactly what I suspected. And he supplied the missing piece - lectins.

This explains why I was close, but no cigar. I still have to minimize exposure to lectins. Once I limit lectins, I should be able to increase saturated fats without having BP spikes.

ETA: I left out an important point. Lectin tries to pry open a hole in your gut lining so that LPS can escape the gut into the blood stream. This is a plant defensive strategy to try to weaken its enemy, us (big insects). LPS is attacked by our white blood cells which results in inflammation.

> Two of the benefits of his diet are normal blood pressure and healthy heart. I think removing lectins and supplementing with vitamin k2 should reverse my heart valve problem.
>
> I have theorized that my BP spikes were caused by eating saturated fat and having undigested fat and bacteria leak into the bloodstream due to poor fat digestion, and that the cure was a low fat diet. ( I have explained this to several MDs, who just shake their head and give me their doctor look. Then they say "You're under-medicated.)
>
> My problem was that low fat worked most of the time, but not all of the time. My daily BP was fine, but I still have dangerously high, unexplained BP spikes. I was close.
>
> A couple of short quotes from Dr Gundry's book ([The Plant Paradox](https://www.amazon.com/Plant-Paradox-Dangers-Healthy-Disease-ebook/dp/B01IMYGX8S/ref=dp_kinw_strp_1)) which explain more scientifically what I was theorizing:
>
> And later discussing a low fat diet:
>
> Dr Gundry confirmed exactly what I suspected. And he supplied the missing piece - lectins.
>
> This explains why I was close, but no cigar. I still have to minimize exposure to lectins. Once I limit lectins, I should be able to increase saturated fats without having BP spikes.

You may be on a good road.
But you must also concentrate on micronutrients that you will be missing doing it.

Spectracell and fatty acid analysis could come handy.

\===================
What is a Pulse?
Pulses, including beans, peas, lentils chickpeas

Well-known legumes include alfalfa, clover, peas, beans, chickpeas, lentils, lupin bean, mesquite, carob, soybeans, peanuts and tamarind.

\====================
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lectin#Lectins\_in\_food](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lectin#Lectins_in_food)

Lectins in food
Lectins are ubiquitous in nature and many foods contain the proteins. Because some lectins can be harmful if poorly cooked or consumed in great quantities, "lectin-free" fad diets have been proposed, most based on the writing of **Steven Gundry**.
**A typical lectin-free diet excludes a range of foods, including
most grains,
pulses and
legumes, as well as
eggs,
seafood and many
staple fruits and vegetables.**
These foods do not contain harmful levels of lectins when properly cooked, and there is no health benefit to following these diets for most people. A strict lectin-free diet is unbalanced and dangerously low in many nutrients, requiring significant dietary supplementation to maintain health.

### Huck — 2017-08-27T16:41:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222359>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=222356)
>
> You may be on a good road.

Dr Gundry recommends using a pressure cooker to deactivate lectins. If I understand correctly, once you eliminate lectins from diet, you may be able to add some foods back in if you don't react to them. Some people are sensitive to almost all foods containing lectins and some aren't.

Eliminating lectins seems to be important for fixing a leaky gut. Once the leaky gut is fixed and the good bacteria in microbiome is replenished, you might be able to handle some lectins.

What he is advocating is not in conflict with what Jack says, just another piece to the puzzle. Actually, he is in agreement with many of the things Jack talks about like EMF, light, fats, and intermittent fasting.

This is one case where it looks like wilipedia has got it wrong. If they are going to use Dr Gundry's name, they should quote accurately what his diet is. It is primarily vegetables, fish, seafood, limited fruit in season, lots of olive oil, certain types of meat and dairy, etc. He talks about foods, like bread, in Europe that are still made the old way, that don't have roundup exposure, and have few lectins. Things like eliminating the skin and seeds of tomatoes to get rid of the lectins.

I wish I had Gundry 20 years ago, but worked through this stuff with the Blood Type stuff and more importantly a food diary.

I think it's a good idea to single out what is ripping you apart (which lectins) by logging what you eat and then logging how you feel the next 24-48 hours. Foods that don't work with me: Wheat, beans, lentils, potatoes.

I put in the work 15 years ago to identify all these things and simplified my regular meals. The good news now is that once every two months, as a redox check, I purposely throw back a bag or chips or have a double cheese burger. When I get good amounts of good old Texas UV and do a 55F cold bath 3x/week my body has zero reaction to these foods. I think that's the "Redox before you detox" line mentioned here.

Still, if you're suspecting your redox is low then do yourself the favor and do map out what foods are permeating your gut and screwing up your system.

### Huck — 2017-08-27T22:02:17+0300

Did a 2 mile walk and thought some more about what this means. I was right about undigested fat and bacteria getting into the blood stream. Where I was wrong was thinking it was caused by the liver, when it was caused by a leaky gut and lectins. That means I can stop liver support supplements.

The leaky gut and lectins also contributed to vitamin d deficiency, so I need to test quarterly to see when I can reduce D3 supplements.

### Huck — 2017-08-28T00:01:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222381>

Walked another 2 miles and had some more insights. One is I need to walk more!

The liver does come into play, but in a slightly different way. It still has to clean up the blood.

I have poor redox, so I would guess that the liver probably does not get all the ATP it needs during the day to be optimal. So when I eat fats, some gets into the blood stream. About 4-5 pm I start experiencing the beginning of the spike. BP keeps rising for several hours.

Now here's the new part. According to Chinese medicine, the liver's time is about 1 am. This corresponds pretty well with the peak of my spike. Then sometime in the next 2 to 3 hours (3-4 am), I see a dramatic drop in BP. I assume this is the liver cleaning the bacteria and undigested fat out of the blood, and then BP returning to normal.

So I guess there is the possibility that if redox was high, the liver could have handled this during the day and not had a spike at all. But instead the liver had to wait until I was asleep and there was atp available for the liver.

I still have a ways to go, but at least I see the path now. Minimize lectins, heal the gut, rebuild the good bacteria, and improve redox.

>
> Still, if you're suspecting your redox is low then do yourself the favor and do map out what foods are permeating your gut and screwing up your system.

I started with the blood type stuff, but then progressed to D'Adamo's follow up, the Genotype diet. With that its taking biometric measurements of torso versus leg length, upper and lower leg bone lengths, fingers, left/right side differences, finger print ridges etc which gives info on the womb experience. Both DS2 and I have the same blood type, O neg, but aren't the same "type". Some "types" can be more than one blood group, the "canary in the coal mine" type could be any blood group. It was surprisingly accurate for both of us. It answered a lot of his food aversions and preferences, his particular sensitivity to fluoride as a small child, a susceptibility to autism ... and mine pointed to a susceptibility to obesity, diabetes and AD. (Others on the list including high BP but those were my focus at the time). The best way to describe it is that the biometric measurements are indicators of the environment faced in the womb, and how that influences gene expression, and how lectins and other inflammatory factors can "turn up the volume." I see it now as a partial picture - there's the light, water, magnetism, heteroplasmy etc and also the microbiome. Yes he was born naturally, and yes he was breastfed, but I've come to the realisation that I couldn't pass on the "right" bacteria if I didn't have it myself ....  And the bifido species naturally decline with age ... and I think a lot of strains are susceptible to nnemfs. He is currently having a course of vaccinations (not vaccinated as a child) before going to uni. At 19 its his decision, but I did get him to agree to separating them out and taking a course of probiotics. Checked up on all the strains in the formula, and we're talking 500 billion CFUs per dose. My thinking is that as the species take hold and influence the microbiome as a whole, they will produce the B vitamins, the butyrate etc and with the right seasonal diet _for him,_ with as much light water and magnetism as, at 19, he'll do without my nagging,  he won't need prebiotics or butyrate supplements. (I think I have to be careful with them because I can't assume they are only feeding the good bacteria ....). So far, for him and me doing it as a hack, I am seriously impressed with the results.

@[Huck](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1100/) [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4772943/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4772943/) Probiotics and blood pressure: current insights

### Huck — 2017-08-28T15:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222407>

I have a 2x risk of Alzheimer's disease, according to my 23andme data. No history in my family that I know of. My mother had 7 sisters and brothers and neither them or her mother had Alzheimer's. None on my father's side. But I guess I still need to take every precaution I can.

I ordered [The End of Alzheimer's: The First Program to Prevent and Reverse Cognitive Decline](https://www.amazon.com/End-Alzheimers-Program-Prevent-Cognitive/dp/0735216207/ref=asap_bc?ie=UTF8) by Dr. Dale Bredesen. I listened to an interview he did with Dr Gundry and there is a great degree of similarity in their findings. No matter what causes inflammation, we have to find the cause and stop it.

The other reason I ordered the book is it lists the labs he uses to predict Alzheimer's in his patients. Most are the standard labs we already have done. With just listening to the interview, I believe his finding also confirm what Jack has said about Alzheimer's. It's just that Dr Gundry and Dr Bredesen explain the concepts in language I understand.

I haven't and likely never will master the language of biology, chemistry, and physics. The good thing is, once I hear or read it in language I understand, I can relate it back to things Jack has written that I didn't quite get.

Why does every discipline have to have their own secret language?

### Huck — 2017-08-28T18:35:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222429>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=222421)
>
> Because Nature works that way.

That doesn't explain why PCs, minicomputers, and mainframes all have different words for the same thing.

Many years ago I taught a class on Adv. Novell Netware Administration to a group of mainframe people who were being laid off because of outsourcing. Their manager had worked a deal for them to get training to prepare them for the Certified Novell Enginerr (CNE) certification, as part of the separation agreement.

A friend of mine had just finished teaching them the basic admin course. I believe they had at least one other class besides that one, so I was teaching the 3rd class.

I started the course and they were completely lost. They had hardly learned anything in the other courses. I quickly figured out it was because they knew mainframe terminology and concepts and the other instructor assumed since they were IT people, that they had a good understanding of PC's.

So I taught the course by saying, for example, "When you need to allocate data files, printers, work files, etc, on a (IBM) mainframe you use JCL. On a PC, you do the same basic functions using a CLIST". So every concept we went over, I related to how they did things on the mainframe.

At the end of the class, the manager came in and asked if I could stay for a few days and reteach them the basic admin class. I was the only instructor they understood. It was like the other instructors were speaking a foreign language.

The lesson I took away from that experience was to teach using analogies whenever possible. People may not know the language, but relate it to something they already know and they get it.

Yes, part of the reason each profession has its own language is to be specific, but part of it is also because of tunneling. Researchers working in a vacuum. I think some of it is also to create a mystique and to protect their profession from the great unwashed.

### Huck — 2017-08-29T18:30:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222520>

If anyone is having trouble staying motivated, let me recommend this video. It scared the crap out of me.

It drove home what Jack says, and I think I finally got it. The problem is not that I am overweight, or that I don't get a great night's sleep, or that my testosterone is low, or whatever. It is the combined damage they cause. That's why Jack focusses on the mitochondria so that the body has the energy to heal itself. Going after each problem separately is a losing battle. With the increasing rate of Alzheimer's, if we don't follow either Jack's or similar protocols, Alzheimer's just might be our future.

Dr Bredesen has identified over 30 factors that lead to Alzheimer's. He says that the number is probably closer to 100. They are the usual culprits we all fight - low vitamin d, high glucose, high insulin, high hs-crp, low testosterone, apo4, estrodial, thyroid, cortisol, stress, sleep, etc. At some point, the brain just starts shutting down functions because it doesn't have the energy to keep them running.

He explains it as 30 holes in your roof. You don't have to plug them all, but you do have to plug the majority of them.

The video helped me realize that I am a prime candidate for Alzheimer's, even though there is no family history. I have the markers that indicate a likelihood of getting it; and at 68, I don't have much time to turn things around.

(You can play at 1.5 speed and still hear clearly)

He has a list of Yes foods and No foods.

His analogy is that plants have defensive mechanisms against predators - primarily insects. The goal of the plant is to reproduce, so anything that threatens the plant's babies must be defended against. One mechanism is poison.

So over time, if we eat enough of a plant, the dose can become toxic. These are plants with lectins, some worse than others.

I recently came up with a new problem. For the last 4 days, my heart rate has been in the 50's most of the day. I actually had 1 reading in the high 40's. I have no idea what caused it to drop 10 points unless it is the K2 I am taking.

### Huck — 2017-09-01T13:39:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222708>

Yesterday was bad and today is starting out the same. Feels like my heart is beating out of my chest. Did have a period where heart rate went from high 60's to 100. Also had BP spike from about 123/80 to 175/95. Also upset stomach and trouble sleeping. When I do fall asleep, I wake up with a hard and rapid heart beat similar to what I had when I was exposed to high emf near my bed.

At first I thought it was just from eating so much greens, but that doesn't account for heart beating so hard. This seems more like an allergic reaction. If I ever ate avocado before it was in guacamole, which I don't like and don't eat. This diet is big on eating avocados and using avocado oil, so I could be allergic. The other possibility is I went from a low fat diet to control BP spikes, to one high in short and medium chain fatty acids. Maybe it's just too much fat too soon. I think this more likely the cause.

I think I can build my own diet that restricts lectins and is lower fat until leaky gut is fixed. Then I can start adding in more fats.

I feel so bad I would go see a doctor, but my guess is that they would just run an EKG and when it comes back normal, they would write a prescription for Nexium.

I think fasting, water, and a potato are my best option. Clear out whatever is causing the problem. I'll throw a baked potato in the refrigerator so it will be largely resistant starch when I eat it.

The only positive is I lost 4 lbs in 2 days.

ETA: One other thing that is strange. My fbg usually is in the 80's. About 2 weeks ago, it suddenly shot up to a a range of 91 - 104. After just 1 day on the cleanse phase of Plant Paradox diet, fbg dropped into the 60's. Both yesterday and today, fbg on waking was 64.

ETA2: Within 1 1/2 hours of eating baked potato, bp was back under 120/80. I think the 4 lb loss in 2 days may have dehydrated me. Going to add in more cruciferous vegetables and see how I react to ghee.

### Huck — 2017-09-03T17:06:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222794>

So is it EMF causing excess calcium in the cells that is causing my high blood pressure? Spikes too?

I read an article that said calcium lowered BP. I thought the last thing I should do is supplement calcium, because it appears mine is not going to bones and teeth like it is supposed to, but into my heart valve and other cells. Then I read in Dr Mercola's daily email a quote from Martin Pall that says:

> _What the \[initial two\] studies showed was that you could block or greatly lower the effects \[of EMF\] by using calcium channel blockers … That was the key observation …_
>
>
> _Now \[I have found\] 26 \[papers\] … They all show that EMFs work by activating what are called voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs). These are channels in the outer membrane of the cell, the plasma membrane that surrounds all our cells. When they’re activated, they open up and allow calcium to flow into the cell. It’s the excess calcium in the cell which is responsible for most if not all of the \[biological effects\].”_​

I remember Jack writing about ccb's for EMF, but I never put it all together. I have Norvasc, but it didn't seem to have much, if any, effect on my blood pressure.

Something else I have been wondering about. The man I bought my current house from was a widower - his wife died from complications from Alzheimers. Now he is in a nursing home with Alzheimers. His sister is about his age and seems fine. She lives in the neighborhood, but about 1/4 mile away. I wonder if there is something about this house that may have led to their Alzheimers?

​

### Huck — 2017-09-04T15:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-222847>

Something I have been wondering about is "Why Not Do Both"?

Why not follow Jack's recommendations for everything but food choices, and substitute a different diet if you believe it is better for your N=1? If that other diet recommends supplements, why not take them? In many cases, the "new" diet can be tailored to imitate the Leptin reset.

It seems to me that doctors like Bredesen and Gundry have used science, lab tests, and patient histories/outcomes to develop diet/supplement plans that appear to work. If reducing lectins in my diet improves my health across the board, why not?

The complaint is they don't focus on light, magnetism, emf, etc. On the other hand, their programs appear to be successful even though they don't focus on those things. Dr Gundry is a famous heart surgeon who found he could heal heart problems through diet and avoid having to operate. Dr Bredesen is having success using diet and supplements to prevent and even reverse Alzheimer's. (I haven't read his book yet, so there is probably more to it.)

So back to my question. Why not do both?

trust but verify comes handy
by now I do not have a problem when deciding about eating or not eating seafood (as recommended by X or Y).
I tested numerous times over many years,
for me there is just too much EPA there and that screws my other stuff.

But, you are rather lean on testing, so it is harder to draw conclusions.

I look at this as at groups of populations.
I am sure some people need seafood and others do not.
Most general is that closer to equator people tend to need more carbs
and farther from equator less carbs more seafood.
Add seasons.
All that with very high individual variability, so without testing one newer know where he stands.

Mother Nature does not care, Mother Nature is about statistics and not individual miserable or happy person.

### Huck — 2017-09-14T19:23:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-223639>

I go to youtube university. I just learned how to replace bushings on my lawn tractor. Replaced the bushings, replaced a worn wheel stem, and replaced the rim and tire. Also learned enough about brickwork to replace some damaged bricks. Last Spring I built a new greenhouse that my neighbors thought came from a kit. Now I'm replacing some damaged subfloor in the bathroom.

I'm also learning how to replace wheel bearings on my travel trailer and am thinking about trying to paint my old Blazer (with an electric spray gun) that I use to tow my trailer.

People keep asking me why I don't just pay someone to do these things, but they are things I wish I had learned how to do years ago. Learning how to do them now exercises my brain and my body.

### Huck — 2017-09-15T17:03:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-223720>

I was going over some of my snps and saw this:

> When people with PPAR gamma and the genotype (C,G) or (G,G) have a low polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fat intake and a high saturated fat intake then their obesity and type 2 diabetes risks are dramatically increased. But when there is a higher mono and polu-unsaturated intake and a lower saturated fat intake, their obesity and type 2 diabetes risk is normal. Polyunsaturated fatty acids activate the PPAR=gamma gene.
>
> ... Perostilbene is a plant compound present in blueberries, cranberries, and almonds has been shown to activate PPAR-gamma (and PPAR-alpha) and lower triglycerides and blood glucose levels.

It took years for me to get there, but trial and error led me to eat limited saturated fat and lots of mono and polyunsaturated fats. I also drink almond milk instead of coconut milk, and blueberries are a big part of my diet.

I think one area where scientific studies are failing us is they ignore the exceptions. For example, if the majority of people thrive on saturated fatty acids, studies may show that saturated fat is good for us, even if for 2 or 3% of the population it is not.

### Huck — 2017-09-27T15:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-224369>

Why does it take a painful lesson for me to finally understand what Jack is telling us?

I started seeing my current doctor about 7 years ago. When she first ran a vitamin d lab, I think it was below 20. I don't have a record of the lab, but I remember it was very low. She started me on supplements and over time I found I had to take 15000 IU/day to get vitamin d above 80. Later that year I noticed that in the summer, I could cut back to 10,000.

She (doctor) over the years would recommend vitamin k, but I don't remember why. I don't think we ever discussed it was needed to balance vitamin d.

Two years ago I had an echocardiogram and learned I had mild aortic stenosis (as). Recently I had another echo and now I have moderate as.

I did my research and learned vitamin K2 and specifically MK-4 and MK-7 can reverse as. So I start taking a high dose of K2 daily,

Last night it dawned on me what likely happened. Vitamin D helps the body absorb calcium, while vitamin K2 makes sure calcium goes to the bones and teeth and not into soft tissue. So a balance of vitamin d and K2 is the ticket. But what I did was supplement vitamin d3 at a high dosage (15000 IUs) for 5 years without the corresponding K2! I absorbed more calcium, but didn't control where it went. And one place it went was my heart valve.

**I may have caused my own heart disease.** I wonder how many other people are facing this same problem by taking Vitamin D without the corresponding Vitamin K?

So how do I reverse it? I started taking K-2 several weeks ago. Doctor concurred and wanted me to up dose to 3000 - 5000 mcg/day. I asked if this was MK-4 or MK-7. She tried to answer, but I could tell she didn't know. She showed me a quote in a book, but it only said K2. From looking back over Jack's recommendation, I believe he is also referring primarily to MK-4 when he says K-2. The LEF Vitamin K is K1 - 1000 mcg, MK-4 - 1000 mcg, and MK-7 - 200 mcg. Jack recommends as high as 5-45 mgs for his bone and heart patients (I think this is MK-4).

I see 2 problems:

1.  Should I greatly decrease Vitamin D supplementation while I try to reverse AS? I temporarily decreased from 15000 to 5000 IUs/day until I find what is the best dose to take why reversing AS. I'm also 50 lbs less than when I first starting taking 15000 IUs, so I probably should have only been taking 5000 IUs over the summer when I was taking 10. Waiting on lab results.

2.  When taking a Vitamin K supplement that includes K1, MK-4, and MK-7, how do I compute dose in 1 capsule? Adding each together doesn't make sense because very little K1 converts to K2 and it appears that only a small amount of MK-4 converts to MK-7.

Based on the answers to these 2 questions, I should be able to calculate a range of doses for K-2. For now, I take 2 LEF Vitamin K capsules in morning and 1 in evening for a total of:

-   K1 - 3000 mcg
-   MK-4 - 3000 mcg
-   MK-7 - 600 mcg

Just from looking at these totals, I will add a 2nd capsule in the evening, but that might still be too low.

### Huck — 2017-10-07T15:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-225196>

When I get a BP spike, I take garlic, potassium, vitamins C and E, and beet root. This is in addition to other supplements I take on a regular basis. I think it helps a little, but doesn't stop or reverse the spike. I think it lowers the peak a little and shortens the duration a little, but it is really hard to tell.

About 2 months ago, I started taking Vitamin K (K1, K2 as MK-4 and MK-7). This seems to correspond to a lowering of overall BP. I also lowered the amount of vitamin D I was taking from 15000 IUs/day to 5000 to keep vitamin d and k in better balance.

I had a BP spike about 10 days ago and again 2 days ago. In addition to the supplements mentioned about, I took a vitamin K capsule and 4 betaine HCL Within an hour, BP was going down and shortly after returned to normal.

So it's too soon to know if this really worked, but I plan to more thoroughly document the next spike and see if in fact either/both the vitamin k and betaine hcl makes a difference.

I tried the vitamin K because I had already seen that it had a lowering effect on bp. The betaine hcl is more just a "gut" response. The BP spikes usually occur after eating too much saturated fat or certain other foods containing wheat. A small amount of wheat doesn't seem to be a problem. So the betaine hcl is to help break down any food that is still in the stomach that might leak through the gut causing a spike in inflammation.

So the hack will be to try each separately and then both together to see which one, if either, makes a difference.

### Huck — 2017-11-01T13:42:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-227078>

I found a sparrow that must have been locked in my garage for at least a day. I thought it was dead, until I picked it up and it moved a little. I took it outside and put it in the sun. I put a lid full of water and some sesame seeds close to it, but it never showed any interest in them. After about 30 minutes in the sun, it started to look around. After another 30 minutes, it flew away.

At almost the same time the sparrow flew away, a hawk chased a woodpecker which then crashed into a garage window. I will leave out the 1st 5 minutes, but eventually the woodpecker was able to turnover onto its belly. It lay still except for gasping for breath. After about 10 more minutes it was breathing a little better, so I moved it to where I had placed the sparrow, in the sun. I didn't expect the woodpecker to make it, but after about 1 1/2 hours, it suddenly flew up high into a nearby tree.

So neither wanted water, but the sun seemed to do the trick. Maybe when people are injured, instead of giving them a glass of water we should quickly get them into the sun.

On my way to better health, I took another painful detour. I've lost about 90 lbs and approaching 70 (age), so I have some sagging skin. After some reading, I decided to add collagen powder. Over the next few days, my BP crept up until it reached 180/110 with a heart rate of 120 (normally in 60's and 70's). I thought it was my usual spike and did the diet and supplements that usually bring it back down, but it only came down a little. And I developed a terrible headache I couldn't shake.

I was worried that this was a new phase in my BP saga and I might have to go on more meds (just 1 now). I thought about what had changed and then I remembered I had started taking collagen. But the articles said it was good for BP. (When I went back and rechecked, it said good for MAINTAINING normal BP). So I looked up collagen and high BP.

> People with high blood pressure have elevated blood levels of collagen, a protein, which may help explain why these individuals are at risk for heart failure as well as kidney and other organ failure, according to a study in the Aug. 11 issue of Circulation: Journal of the American Heart Association. [https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/1998/08/980817081222.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/1998/08/980817081222.htm)

So no collagen for last 2 days and seem to be returning to normal.

ETA: Just checked BP and it has returned to normal, so about 48 hours after stopping collagen.

### Huck — 2017-12-17T12:57:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-230481>

Last winter, I spent several weeks in my travel trailer on the Gulf coast. On 2 occasions I woke up with heart palpitations. On those 2 nights, and only those nights, I had used a small electric heater. The electric supply (110 vac) for the trailer was directly under my head when sleeping. All the slack power cable was also coiled up under my head. I decided to disconnect the power cable (shore power) at night and never had the problem again.

I was pretty sure I would get pushback if I posted my experience on an RV site where I am a member, so I ended up not posting it until now. Sure enough, a couple of guys with EE degrees said the heart palpitations could not have been caused by ac power. Within 24 hours the thread was deleted with no explanation given. According to site rules, they are supposed to delete inflammatory posts, not the entire thread. So far, my request for an explanation has gone unanswered.

When I returned home, I used my Trifield meter to verify the high emf. I previously had the same problem at home when using an electric heater in the bedroom. I solved it by moving the bed about a foot from the wall. In my trailer, I reversed the head and foot of bed to get my head and heart further away from power. I will also start using my propane heater instead of the electric heater at night.

There is an entire class of trailers built this way with the power connection directly under the bed (4"-6"). I bet quite a few people have had the same problem with heart palpitations that I had. It's really a shame my experience/solution won't be available to them.

### Huck — 2017-12-26T17:07:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-231098>

I'll be 69 in a couple of weeks and suddenly really feeling it. My BP soared right at the Winter Solstice like it has for the previous 2 years. Three days of primarily eating baked potatoes got it back down to normal. Prostate is swollen and causing hip pain. I've been able to reduce inflammation some, but not enough. Sleep is not good primarily because of extremely frequent nocturnal urination, which after 2 really bad nights is now back to 2 x a night. My sinuses always get congested in winter and this winter is no exception even though I started using a humidifier with lavender oil. Eating the same as I was in summer and fall, but I have gained about 15 lbs in last 2 months.

I need major change. Tweaking diet isn't going to be enough. I need a better plan. My biggest hurdle is saturated fat. A small amount is OK, but at some level (something over 10-20 g) my BP spikes. Coconut oil is a problem, as is butter. The jury is out on MCT oil as it doesn't have long-chain fatty acids, like coconut oil does. I use a lot of EVOO, sesame oil, and avocado oil.

I'm working on developing a 90 day plan to cure a lot of what ails me. I read several recently published books on Alzheimer's, lectins, Buddhism, and meditation. I looked up foods, spices, and supplements that help with heart disease, blood pressure, cancer, prostate problems, weight loss, memory, etc. Then I did the same by category: anti-inflammatory, anti-fungal, anti-bacterial, anti-cancer.

What I found interesting is that there is about a 80% - 90% similarity in Jack's Epi-Paleo, Dr. Gundry's Plant Paradox, and Dr Bredesen's The End of Alzheimer's. They get there different ways, but the food list is very similar. Other books I read were based on a vegetarian solution. What I am starting to see many youtube doctors moving to is a combination of vegetarian for part of the week and then adding seafood, fish, or grass fed meats for the rest of the week. Most recommend intermittent fasting and occasional water fasting.

I think I have the food list under control. The actual menu is going to take some work to get the variation I need. I will probably add a green drink because I always seem to fail at eating enough greens. I can also dump some spices in the drink to make sure I get enough of them, which is usually another fail.

I drink about a half gallon of water a day, and tried to increase it to a gallon. The reasons were to see if it might help lower my BP and also to see if it would reduce prostate inflammation and frequent urination. I couldn't drink a gallon. I'm sure I could have forced it down, but the extra water was adding to my frequent urination problem to the point I was going every 30 minutes. I'm guessing that I am dehydrated and need to be able to drink more water. This seems to be worse in the winter than in the summer and I bet Jack already posted the science of behind it! So I need to research this more and figure out what I need to do to get properly hydrated.

The 2 books I am reading on meditation are [**Radical Acceptance: Embracing Your Life With the Heart of a Buddha**](<https://www.amazon.com/Radical-Acceptance-Embracing-Heart-Buddha/dp/0553380990/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1514300495&sr=1-1&keywords=Radical Acceptance: Embracing Your Life With the Heart of a Buddha>) and **[Why Buddhism is True: The Science and Philosophy of Meditation and Enlightenment](https://www.amazon.com/Why-Buddhism-True-Philosophy-Enlightenment/dp/1439195455/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1514300567&sr=1-1&keywords=Why+Buddhism+is+True%3A+The+Science+and+Philosophy+of+Meditation+and+Enlightenment).** They wear me out. I can't read more than a few pages before I have to put them down. I have only read a few chapters, but I already find it having a calming effect on me. I actually can stop my mind from wandering for a few minutes.

I am also developing an exercise program I can stick to. Goals are to increase flexibility, improve endurance and heart health, improve core strength, and improve overall strength. I recently started Tai Chi and that is something I can stick with. I think adding walking and core strength are good goals for the 1st month. I'll revise exercise program after 30 days.

Sun light is something I fail at in winter. I plan on spending at least 30 days camping on Gulf coast in my trailer, so that makes light and grounding natural to do without even thinking about it. I will try to use that to get some good habits started.

Since I was a kid, I have always had to do things my way. The way my brain seems to work is I have to get everything to make sense before I can adopt it. For example, when I was in a COBOL class, the instructor gave us a sort subroutine to use, but I had to write my own so I could understand how it actually worked. The same with a diet. I can't just follow the Epi-Paleo diet unless I own it. And to own it, my brain has to put the pieces together in a way that makes sense to me. This is both a burden and a blessing. It takes me much longer to learn something, but once I get it, I own it.

I have to put together my diet/nutrient plan, my exercise plan, my environmental plan (light, grounding, emf, etc), my anti-stress plan, and my anti-cancer/anti-Alzheimer's plan, so that I can own it.

Suggestions always welcome.

ETA: Just remembered that the one thing that worked for me in the past was to measure FBG every morning when I get up. By driving FBG down into 60's and 70's, I was able to lose a great deal of weight and felt fantastic. So that needs to be a major part of my plan.

How much MK4 and MK7 are you taking daily? You need a high dosage to reverse the calcium buildup. Vitamin K and vitamin D work together, so make sure you keep your D level in upper range. I'm taking LEF Vitamin K which is K1 - 1000 mcg, MK-4 - 1000 mcg, and MK-7 - 200 mcg. I take 1 in morning and 1 in afternoon.

When I told my doc what I was doing and why, she was already familiar with using vitamin K for AS and said she had successfully treated a few older patients with vitamin K. Unfortunately she has had to close her office for at least 6 months, so I don't know how well the vitamin K is working so far. She did a very thorough exam checking heart beat and stenosis and noting how strong it was, so she had a baseline for next appointment.

I could go in for another ultrasound, but I haven't been able to find a cardiologist that believes vitamin K can reverse AS. I've seen 3 and they all say, come back in 6 months and they will do another ultrasound. They offer absolutely no treatment to try to reverse it.

Check this out as well. [https://www.jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/](https://www.jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/)

### Huck — 2018-11-10T19:01:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-255404>

Haven't updated for several months.

My aortic stenosis appears to be getting worse. While I don't have chest pain, I do feel something in my heart and it is occurring much more frequently. I can't describe the feeling, but normally one doesn't even notice their heart, and the thing is, sometimes I do.

I will feel good foe several days, highly motivated, get a lots of things done, and then I crash and just want to sleep for 2 or 3 days. I don't have dizziness or fainting, but it's not unusual for someone with severe aortic stenosis to be asymptomatic. After 3 echocardiograms they still can't tell what type of valve I have (bicuspid or tricuspid).

I switched to a Cardiologist at Duke University Hospital. My new cardiologist ordered a NT-pro-BNP, which showed the left ventricle under normal (or low flow) level, which is why I don't have symptoms. He also did a BMP and ECG which were both normal.

I'm scheduled for a cardio stress test and a CT TAVR PROTOCOL INC CT AB PELVIS AND CT HRT W SURG PLNG (CT scan) which will hopefully determine if I have a bicuspid or tricuspid valve.

A bicuspid valve is genetic and often has to be replaced at a much younger age than I am. although it is not uncommon for someone in their 60's or 70's to have a bicuspid valve replaced. A tricuspid valve is what 95%+ of the population has. More and more people in their 70' and 80's are experiencing calcification of the aortic valve to the point it must be replaced.

Until recently there were 2 options: do nothing and die or open heart surgery (OHS) to replace the valve. The OHS option is know as SAVR (Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement).

Fairly recently a new option has been developed called TAVR (Transcatheter aortic valve replacement). In TAVR, the most common approach is to make a small incision in the leg and insert a catheter into the artery, guide the catheter to the heart, and will replace the bad valve with the new valve.

TAVR started as an option for patients with severe aortic stenosis that were considered a bad risk for OHS. It proved very successful, so TAVR was made optional for people at moderate risk. Again, it has proved successful. Unfortunately, TAVR has not been approved for low risk surgical patients, which is what I am.

There is a trial available, where half the participants get TAVR and half get SAVR. That's better than no chance for TAVR, but I still don't want OHS if there is a possibility of avoiding it. I'm pretty sure the trial is only for people with tricuspid valves.

Until recently, TAVR was not available for people with a bicuspid aortic valve, which is why I am pushing my doctor to determine which type of valve I have. I think when they are doing SAVR, it really doesn't make much difference, because they just cut the old valve out and it doesn't make much difference if it is bicuspid or tricuspid. I want to know because there is a new trial just starting that is just for TAVR with bicuspid valves on low surgical risk patients. So while there is an increased risk of going with TAVR for a bicuspid valve, I think I am willing to accept the risk to avoid OHS.

On the positive side, I just had another MRI for prostate cancer which was negative. So an MRI about 5 years ago and this one and no cancer detected.

I can't find a single verified study or even an MD that has provided labs that show anyone has been able to reduce or eliminate valve calcification. Arteries yes, but valves No.

I asked the last 2 cardiologists about calcium test and both said it wouldn't help with valve calcification. Again, I can find no evidence that calcium in the aortic valve can be removed by any means other than surgery. However, they have found that when they use TAVR (which inserts a new valve into the old valve and leaves debris behind), somehow the debris gets cleaned out without causing a problem. So it seems the body has a mechanism that might be used to get at the calcium.

There are several markers for valve calcification - HDL, LP(a), Homocysteine are the 3 I can think of. My HDL is 61, LP(a) is good, but homocysteine is high. I am taking betaine, methyl folate, methyl B12, krill oil, magnesium, eating spinach and broccoli, and exercising to bring down homocysteine. I think a genetic methylation problem may be part of the reason I haven't been able to lower homocysteine. I still take vitamin K (K1, K2 MK4 and MK7) plus D3, but if anything the calcification seems to have gotten worse over the last year.

I think I'm past the point where anything other than valve replacement is an option. Once it is severe, which mine is, on average death occurs within 2 years if the valve is not replaced.

If I end up with OHS, I will be asking for some help on what to do to recover as quickly as possible.

He doesn't mention aortic valve anywhere on his web site. If he had a cure for removing plaque from the aortic valve, he would have it plastered all over his web site. I have already tried most of what he recommends and over the last year, the valve opening has gotten much smaller indicating plaque is getting worse.

If there was a method to remove plaque from the aortic valve, someone would have posted it on youtube. There is one chiropractor that claims he has reduced aortic valve plaque, but all his references are to removing it from the artery, not the valve. I have done pubmed searches, youtube searches, google searches, searched different health websites, and can't find any reference to successfully removing valve plaque.

What I hoped was that since aortic valve cells are constantly being created, that if I could remove the cause of the calcification and provide plenty of HDL for cleanup, that as the valve was rebuilt it would be rebuilt without plaque. Either I haven't removed the cause of the plaque buildup or my assumption is wrong.

I do have a question for you. Cold increases blood viscosity, raises blood pressure, and causes heart attacks, especially in the elderly.

"Temperature has a profound, direct effect on the viscosity of blood. A good rule of thumb is: Each 1°C reduction in temperature causes the viscosity of blood to increase by about 2 percent. Blood viscosity is an emerging cardiovascular risk factor, which is highly sensitive to cold temperatures and explains the increased cardiovascular risk during the winter months. Marc Claeys, the cardiovascular researcher at the University of Antwerp in Belgium who led an authoritative study analyzing data from 107,090 European participants, said, “Increased platelet aggregation and blood viscosity during cold exposure promotes thrombosis and clot formation.”1 [http://www.bloodflowonline.com/perspectives/can-cold-blood-stop-your-heart](http://www.bloodflowonline.com/perspectives/can-cold-blood-stop-your-heart)"
​

My blood pressure always goes up in winter which I thought was from less sunlight, but it looks like it is sunlight and cold.

Here's the question: If one is cold adapted, would cold have the same effect?

Marc Claeys, the cardiovascular researcher at the University of Antwerp in Belgium who led an authoritative study analyzing data from 107,090 European participants, said, “Increased platelet aggregation and blood viscosity during cold exposure promotes thrombosis and clot formation.”1 [http://www.bloodflowonline.com/perspectives/can-cold-blood-stop-your-heart](http://www.bloodflowonline.com/perspectives/can-cold-blood-stop-your-heart)"
> ​
>
> My blood pressure always goes up in winter which I thought was from less sunlight, but it looks like it is sunlight and cold.
>
> Here's the question: If one is cold adapted, would cold have the same effect?

I would contact dr Dach,
have consultation with him.
Preferably you (after paying $$) send him e-mail with questions he answers in writing.
But the question would have to be simple and to the point.
Similar to:
I have calcification on my aortic heart valve (and likely aortas),
will use of Bolouke and antibiotics help in removing calcification?
If yes, please advice on my next move.
If no, do you have alternative to surgery?

...................
About blood viscosity.
It goes up when its temperature goes down, blood pressure goes up in attempt to compensate for the flow.

Applicable to people who are not able to hold proper body temperature.
\----------------------------------------
About cold adapted
those peoples body temperature does not go down in cold.
Those people's blood is re-directed toward surface based on higher heat demand.
Those peoples freeT3 goes up in cold to accommodate higher metabolism that results in supplying higher heat.

I wish I knew why calcification of valve is different from artery, but it is. I joined Dr Davis's site for a while to see if he had any info on removing calcium from valve, but he doesn't. At one time he said 2 people had reported to him an increase in valve opening after taking vitamin K, but then never mentioned it again.

I think what probably happened is echocardiograms are not very good at showing valve opening size (a calculation), and it was probably just a variance in echos that showed what appeared to be an improvement. Even 2 cardiologists who reviewed the same echo would likely calculate different sizes.

I have searched for others reporting vitamin k helps with aortic stenosis and I found nothing; although there is some evidence that suggests it may be helpful for arterial calcification.

### Huck — 2019-02-06T14:46:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-262396>

Cleveland heart Lab has a heart disease inflammation panel. If interested call Cleveland Heart to purchase and then go to Quest for blood draw. Results in about 10 days. [http://www.knowyourrisk.com/](http://www.knowyourrisk.com/)

• F2-Isoprostanes (F2-IsoPs)
• Oxidized Low-Density Lipoprotein (OxLDL)
• ADMA/SDMA
• Microalbumin
• High Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hsCRP)
• Lp-PLA2 Activity• Myeloperoxidase (MPO)

I just got my results and I find them confusing. Every single lab shows low risk for cardiovascular disease, yet I have severe aortic stenosis and 2 partially blocked arteries. Maybe I got the plaque first and then cleared up inflammation after.

The one problem I know I have is high homocysteine. I watched a video yesterday of a doctor reviewing current studies n homocysteine and heart disease and he said they found that reducing homocysteine with supplements did not reduce risk of heart disease.

I've been doing much better at getting more winter sun, wearing blue blockers at night, and grounding.

### Huck — 2019-02-06T15:19:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-262398>

This post should have been posted before previous post.

I had a cardiac cath about 3 weeks ago. I was diagnosed with severe aortic stenosis, so I thought this was the last procedure that was needed before having TAVR (Transcather Aortic valve Replacement).

About an hour after the cath, my cardiologist and one that did the cath came into recovery room and told me I had blockage in blah blah blah. They seemed to lapse into a foreign language at that point. The only word I understood was 70%. I've been trying ever since to get my cardiologist to tell me exactly what's blocked and percent of blockage, and getting nowhere.

Then they said I needed either stents and a new aortic valve by catheter through the leg or bypass surgery and open heart surgery to replace the valve. They gave me (and my sister who was with me) the impression this was urgent. Then they sent me for an echocardiogram.

The cath procedure caused my bladder to spasm, so I had the urge to pee but couldn't. My BP and heart rate also jumped up to very high levels and I had trouble with the technical information they were telling me. As my cardiologist was leaving the room, he said he was sending the surgeon in to talk to me. A little later the surgeon came in and didn't really have any idea why he was talking to me. He had talked to my cardiologist and new my diagnosis, but no surgery was planned at that point, so he really had nothing to add.

I have the echo and learn that my aortic valve area (ava) is 1.1 cm2. This was interesting because what had caused the diagnosis of severe aortic stenosis 4 months ago was an ava of .77. Now either my ava is getting larger, which is extremely unlikely, or the the interpretation of previous echo was way off - .77 indicates severe aortic stenosis and 1.1 moderate. The cardiac cath also showed an ava of 1.1.

Three days later I get a message saying they had discussed my case and decided it wasn't urgent and to come back in 5 months. I've yet to get an explanation of why it changed from urgent to not urgent, what arteries are blocked and the severity, and what do they recommend for reducing plaque in arteries. I'm already on my 5th cardiologist, so I have my doubts changing again will make much difference.

So that's why I had the inflammation lab I referenced in above post. The only direction from cardiologist was take a baby aspirin and 40 mg Crestor. Crestor is used to reduce inflammation and my lab shows I already have a low level of inflammation. I see my integrative doc tomorrow and she can help me develop a strategy to reduce arterial plaque.

What is it with cardiologists that they can't communicate and don't seem to have a clue on anything but statins and surgery.

Since I'm venting, from what I am reading, the carotid intima–media thickness (CIMT) echo lab seems to be the best at identifying arterial plaque and percent of blockage. No radiation, so can repeat every few months to see if strategy is working. I called imaging center in my city and they didn't know what it was, same for an imaging center in larger city, and searching web found closest clinic 5 hours away. It's almost like they don't want us to get better!

### Sue-UK — 2019-02-08T12:23:31+0200
