**TULIP, or, The Ultimate Laser Protocol**

Alright, another quick update. I don't know if this is all placebo or coincidence, or if it is just a transient effect from LLLT that I'll have to do weekly to maintain, but I've been feeling pretty dang good the past 5 days (since my first session of putting a red LED to my forehead and starting TULIP). So far I have noticed a large reduction in anxiety and an increase in self-confidence and motivation, also an optimistic outlook on things as well. Not to mention my fiance and brother have both commented on my seemingly drastic change in aura and self-presentation - I'm buzzing with mental energy. Random memories have came back, I used the ROYGBIV saying (red, orange, yellow, etc.) to help describe the visual light spectrum to my fiance and how red light specifically has such drastic effects on physiology. Thoughts have been flowing so quickly and they just literally sort themselves out without any effort. It's like the pieces to a puzzle being put together by itself, only in my mind. I don't know if this has to do with the LLLT, or TULIP, or the combination.

For anyone interested in the application of transcranial photobiomodulation, I recommend reading this amazing review: [http://pdfcast.org/pdf/suck-it-trebek](http://pdfcast.org/pdf/suck-it-trebek) (you may have to download this and I don't know if that somehow violates anything on these boards - please I'm not spamming, fishing, trying to advertise, etc. thi pdf contains the most up to date literature review by the experts in the area of the transranial applications of red lasers and LED's). This is also an interesting study: [http://dspace.mit.edu/handle/1721.1/58558#files-area](http://dspace.mit.edu/handle/1721.1/58558#files-area).

I would love to understand how the field comes into play here. Since it affects literally everything, I wonder how red lasers/LEDs can affect the field, and I wonder if you are living in an alright field, if LLLT will aid in recovery or performance even more. This is a great guide set up by a fellow bio-hacker I've been in contact with: [http://www.longecity.org/forum/user/11887-lostfalco/](http://www.longecity.org/forum/user/11887-lostfalco/) he is the one who sent me the LED arrays as well as his TULIP stack and the two books be recommends. The books seem absolutely phenomenal and anyone reading Dr. Kruse' blogs must get them. They describe the interconnectedness of the macroscopic and microscopic world in great detail, as well as conservation of energy/energy transfers. It gives a good visual of what is happening with our mitochondria and how transcranial photobiomodulation affects them.

\*\*\*MAJOR POINT\*\*\* I firmly believe Dr. Kruse is onto something with all his work on 'the field' and magnetism and water. I'm not saying that people should just get a red laser and say screw grounding and avoiding artificial light, etc. But, if this is no coincidental few days of strong well-being, or placebo, then this is truly giving me a shot at getting my life in order. I seriously didn't know how I would finish this upcoming semester of school with my HPPD, hence why most of my courses are online (which I regret now), and there was no way I'd be able to attain another job. Now, I feel similar to how I did before I first experienced even minimal HPPD symptoms - only with a mass of knowledge behind me and quite possibly increasingly enhanced mitochondria.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-09-02T23:44:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tdcs-and-my-existentialist-state-of-mind.7760/post-94703>

Some more information and an update:

I really, REALLY recommend the following two books: The Machines Of Life, by David S. Goodsell; it explains a lot of cellular/molecular biology in thorough but simple terms with lots of colored graphics. It's short and to the point. I've only read the first chapter, but will definitely finish the book this week - after, I plan on going back through some of the foundational blogs, especially regarding mitochondria. The second book is Life's Ratchet: How Molecular Machines Extract Order From Chaos, by Peter M. Hoffman. Another good book on cellular/molecular biology, but a bit more in depth and with a lot of citations. These books seem foundational to understanding some of the biological mechanisms and reactions going on in my current experiments with LLLT/TULIP.

Update:
3rd session with 48 red LED light to my forehead, directly before bed. Did it for only about 1.5 minutes at both regions. Felt normal, in fact slightly relaxed afterwards. Fell asleep quickly and woke up refreshed. Woke up, had some chocolate and tea, took my TULIP stack, and did some short CT. I plan on finishing another chapter or 2 of The Machines of Life today outside in the sun while grounding with intermittent Buteyko Breathing/meditation. Also gonna do some Tabata sprints - I will probably have consumed more carbs today due to the "holiday" and enjoying a BBQ with my family, so I may as well cause a glycogen deficit, lol. Don't worry, though, I'll be eating pretty much epi-paleo: A pound or so of fish with a bunch of guacamole, roasted onions and garlic and tomatoes, cilantro, maybe find a way to incorporate some lime/lemon juice/rind, etc. I feel a little bit off today, but at the same time more motivated and optimistic. Probably the hormetic affect of the LEDs done only about 13hrs ago.

Found some more interesting information on IR LEDs/Lasers for health, recovery, and performance. I'm not going to copy the entire post because it is far too long, but it is interesting and if anyone wants to know more I can post the rest or post a link:

"Copyright 2004 James Clayton Roberts

What is the Euon?

The name, Euon, comes from the prefix "eu" , which means "good or normal" , and "on" from "photon" , a wave-particle of light

The Euon is an infrared (IR) light. It is, in part, a special LED that uses an alloy of aluminum, gallium and arsenic to turn electricity into a specific wavelength of light, which is 880 nanometers (billionths of a meter) in length. The LED employs three "chips" of this alloy encased in an epoxy lens, mounted on a steel TO-66 case. The case and the electrodes are gold-plated, to avoid corrosive damage with age. The LED is large, with an active surface area of 7/16 inch (.4375 inch, about 11 millimeters) . This LED is much more powerful than the ones used in television remote controls, etc.

The Euon's output power is 170 mW/Steradians, which is a measure including the beam angle as a factor. It is difficult to represent light output power in the same way we would electricity. The average output power of the Euon's LED is 53 mW. The output from the LED spreads from the active surface area at 120 degrees, a third of a circle.

A small amount of the Euon's light output is slightly longer or shorter than 880 nanometers in wavelength.

The LED is attached to a heat sink (a piece of aluminum) especially made for it's case style, which increases the life of the LED. The heat sink, as with those inside a computer, draws heat away from the LED and radiates into the surrounding air. The case allows maximum airspace around the heat sink for greater efficiency.

The light from the Euon is steady and constant. It is not modulated.

What is the Euon's purpose? What is it good for?

The Euon is what is called a "mind machine" , although it has many more uses over parts of the body other than the brain. A mind machine is an electronic device which uses some form of magnetic, electromagnetic and/or sound energy to alter or enhance awareness. Other mind machines use flashing LEDs in goggles or eyeglass frames, headphones, electrodes to deliver low-level electricity, electromagnets, or biofeedback (EEG, etc.) to manifest positive effects in the user. The Euon is the first infrared light mind machine. The Euon enhances mental and physical function. The specifics, in myself and those who have used the device, are:

(...)" goes on to list some benefits, then:

"Other effects of infrared light (from 660 to 880 nm) that have been documented in many scientific studies are:

1\. Increased ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate) synthesis in cells. ATP synthesis is a measure of the vitality of cells. ATP is involved in most chemical reactions involving energy production in cells.

2\. Increased growth rate of new cells, and an increase in the numbers of new cells.

3\. Greater RNA and DNA production. In a patent for a nootropic (a drug that enhances cognition), a doctor named Glatzky posited eloquently the theory that RNA production in brain cells is the substrate of memory, with documentation for this theory.

4\. Angiogenesis stimulation. Angiogenesis is the formation of new capillaries, which are the smallest blood vessels. This improves microcirculation in the area of IR application.

5\. Reduces swelling and edema through enhanced blood and lymph flow.

6\. Inhibition of the firing of non-adrenergic and non-cholinergic nerves, through nitric oxide release. Firing in these nerves is how non-sensory pain from internal organs is mediated.

7\. An increase in collagen synthesis.

8\. Vasodilation.

I must reiterate that I am stating the above effects as effects of infrared light in general, for educational purposes only, and I am Not claiming that the Euon treats disease or heals."

This is only about half of the post because it is very descriptive, gives contact information, information on how to use it (even for sexual performance, lol), why to not use it near or at the eyes or thyroid or areas that stimulate the amygdala, and then cites a bunch of studies on wound healing and ROS attenuation from IR lasers.

If I had the money, I'd get some safety goggles, the VetroLaser, The Euon, and some less powerful 'pen lasers' to use up my nostril and I'd laser everything! Lol. Again, I'm not neglecting the fact that Magnetism and water and cold and sun are all essential (and primordial). I actually plan to drastically ramp up CT, and consume as much RO water and salt as possible, if it comes down to my last dollars, they are going to clean water rather than even food, I can handle a fast, if I have to I will, lol.

Hope someone, anyone(!!) can give me some input on anything. Thanks, happy Memorial Day to those of you who celebrate it.

I just posted the above bibliography on the Circadian Biohackers group. I suggest everyone look at as much information in that web page as they can absorb. My mind is blown. Watch this interview with independent scientist Barrie Trower: [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rjyfpVRPUVQ#t=220](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rjyfpVRPUVQ#t=220) Notice how around six or eight minutes he starts speaking about how dramatic the effects of EMFs are epigenetically (although he never uses the word). I'm not gonna lie, the internet is a cool thing - it's an international highway system similar to that of an ant colony (or something, lol) connecting us all across the world. I used to be a hardcore gamer - in fact, I plan on getting the new GTA V video game and I will probably be temporarily addicted, lol. But this is amazing information with far reaching implications. I'm going to dedicate all my time and energy to learn and absorb all of this as well as I can, along with the LLLT.

This has overwhelming implications. Not only that, like Dr. Kruse said, people want results yesterday (somewhere in the CT-series), well, I believe that goes for negative effects as well. People put their hands on a computer or microwave their food and they don't eat it and die or melt into a puddle of nothingness, so they assume it mustn't be all that bad, let alone that Big Pharma, large corporations, the government, etc. all bank on this.

### Luella — 2013-07-31T13:45:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.7761/post-91244>

Hi Jack, I recently started to supplement with magnesium and wondered if it might help a history of facial blushing from an early age and Reynaud's in my right hand? I have researched surgery for this condition but wanted to know if there were alternatives before going down this route? I haven't yet managed to do a full two weeks of Leptin Reset but plan to give it another go.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-31T14:46:57+0300

Yes magnesium can sometimes help both of these conditions.

# Thread: Carbon nanotubes you say?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbon-nanotubes-you-say.7768/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-01T02:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbon-nanotubes-you-say.7768/post-91299>

[http://www.forskningsradet.no/en/Newsarticle/Cell\_signals\_via\_membrane\_nanotubes/1253966097275](http://www.forskningsradet.no/en/Newsarticle/Cell_signals_via_membrane_nanotubes/1253966097275)

Most of the bodyâ€™s cells communicate with each other by sending electrical signals through nano-thin membrane tubes. A sensational Norwegian research discovery may help to explain how cells cooperate to develop tissue in the embryo and how wounds heal.

For nearly ten years, researchers have known that cells can â€œgrowâ€ ultra-thin tubes named tunnelling nanotubes (TNTs) between one another. These nanotubes â€“ the length of two to three cells and just 1/500th the thickness of a human hair â€“ are connections that develop between nearly all cell types to form a communication channel different from any previously known mechanisms.

Dr. Xiang Wang and Professor Hans-Hermann Gerdes of the University of Bergen have discovered a previously unknown communication system between cells. (Photo: UiB)
In 2010, Dr. Xiang Wang and Professor Hans-Hermann Gerdes â€“ colleagues at the University of Bergenâ€™s Department of Biomedicine â€“ discovered that electrical signals were being passed through nanotubes from one cell to another at high speed (roughly 1-2 m/sec). Their research receives funding under the Research Councilâ€™s large-scale research programme Nanotechnology and New Materials (NANOMAT).

The breakthrough
In their key experiment, Dr Wang used fluorescent dye that changes in intensity as the electric potential of the cell membrane changes. When two cells connected by forming a nanotube, he poked into one of them with a microinjection needle to depolarise that cellâ€™s membrane potential. This caused the fluorescent indicator on the cell membrane to light up like a firework, and it was soon followed by a similar light display in the cell on the other end of the nanotube.
The breakthrough discovery began with an experiment demonstrating intercellular transmission of electrical signals via nanotubes in 2007. The researchers then carried out similar trials with a number of other cell types, observing similar occurrences.

â€œWe confirmed that this is a common phenomenon between cells,â€ explains Professor Gerdes. â€œStill, this characteristic is not in every cell type.â€

The experiment was replicated a number of times to obtain statistically reliable data. The electrophysiology group at the University of Bergen took precise conductivity measurements of the cell systems to determine the strength of the electrical coupling. In autumn 2010 the results were published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS) and were highlighted as top news in Nature News

Here a cell has coupled with another cell by growing a long nanotube which enables it to exchange electrical signals. (Photo: UiB)
Short lifespan
Intercellular nanotubes are far from permanent. Most of them last only a few minutes. This means the researchers cannot predict where and when the cells will form nanotube connections.

â€œIt is truly painstaking work,â€ says Professor Gerdes. â€œYou may sit there examining cells for hours through a microscope without seeing a single tube. If you are lucky, however, you catch sight of a nanotube being created and can film the event.â€

To raise the likelihood of finding nanotubes, the researchers developed a micro-matrix consisting of thousands of points and bridges on a plate surface. Smaller than a postage stamp, the plate is covered by a nano-structured material to which the cells adhere. The researchers place one cell onto each point and hope that nanotubes will form along the bridges between the points. The camera is focused on these bridges.
Once the nanotubes have been established, the researchers manipulate the cells at specified times; meanwhile the microscope is programmed to photograph, say, 50 preselected points every five minutes. The team can thus obtain data about many nanotube connections in a short time.

How do cells do this?
Dr. Wang quickly discovered that the mere presence of a nanotube was not sufficient to transmit an electrical signal. There had to be another mechanism involved as well.

Many cells form tiny membrane pores with each other called gap junctions, which are made up of ring-shaped proteins. Back in the 1960s it was discovered that directly adjacent cells could exchange electrical impulses through these gap junctions. What Dr Wang found was that one end of the nanotube was always connected to cells by a gap junctions before it transmitted its electrical impulses.

He also found that in some coupled cells voltage-gated calcium channels were involved in the forwarding of the incoming signals. When the electrical signal being sent through the nanotube reaches the membrane of the receiving cell, the membrane surface is depolarised, opening the calcium channel and allowing calcium â€“ a vital ion in cell signalling â€“ to enter.

â€œIn other words,â€ explains Professor Gerdes, â€œthere are two components: a nanotube and a gap junction. The nanotube grows out from one cell and connects to the other cell through a gap junction. Only then can the two cells be coupled electrically.â€

Controls embryonic cells?
Now the scientists are seeking answers as to why the cells send signals to each other in this way.

â€œItâ€™s quite possible that the discovery of nanotubes will give us new insight into intercellular communication,â€ asserts Professor Gerdes. â€œThe process could explain how cells are coordinated during embryo growth. In that phase cells travel long distances â€“ yet they demonstrate a kind of collective behaviour, and move together like a flock of birds can.â€

Nanotubes may also be a factor in explaining cell movement associated with wound healing, since cells move toward a wound in order to close it. We already know that electrical signals are somehow involved in this process; scientists can only speculate as to whether nanotubes are involved here as well, stresses Professor Gerdes.

Perhaps brain cells, too?
In terms of electronic signal processing, the human brain surpasses all other organs. If this same signalling mechanism proves to be present in human brain cells, it could add a new dimension to understanding how the brain functions. Communication channels involving synapses and dendrites that are already identified differ widely from nanotubes.
The Bergen-based neuroscientists see this research as an opportunity to formulate better explanations for phenomena related to consciousness and electrical connections in the brain. In the project â€œCell-to-cell communication: Mechanism of tunnelling nanotube formation and functionâ€, they are now studying precisely how nanotube mechanisms function in brain cells.

Professor Gerdes is currently conducting research at the European Molecular Biology Laboratory in Heidelberg. By studying the electrical connections in vivo he hopes to figure out how the mechanisms work in live subjects. The results could enhance understanding of diseases that occur when cell mechanisms fail to function properly.

Without free T3 from iron guess what happens?

Impaired ketone synthesis is one aspect of brain development adversely affected by hypothyroidism of any cause.

Connecting any dots now hypothyroidism leads to fat depletion in the brain long term?

Anyone see the link to neuro-degeneration? Diabetes? Leptin resistance?

since we are on an energy kick on the blog........ya know the whole E=MC2 thing........did you know that the auditory cortex and inferior colliculus of the human brain is the biggest energy hog in the entire body? It uses more Oxygen than any region. In fact, Ammon's horn in the temporal lobe is a marker for oxygen loss from poor mitochondrial function or environmental supply. In fact, here is another unusual fact of humans alone.......hypothyroidism causes serious hearing impairment in us by impeding ossification of the organ of Corti in the inner ear. This is why adult humans who are energy starved often complain of tinnitus.......See, E=MC2. And now you know why tinnitus really occurs. Tinnitus tells us the brain is starved of energy

As products of fatty acid oxidation, long-term ketone availability is directly linked to subcutaneous fat stores. Unlike glucose synthesis, ketone synthesis does not draw on amino acids so it does not threaten organ integrity by depleting tissue protein levels. Brain uptake of ketones occurs through the monocarboxylic acid transporter which rapidly ramps up ketone transport in proportion to rising ketone levels in the blood. The ketones main goal is to provide the brain lactate.

Neurons in the CNS and PNS love lactate, especially when they are suboptimal in ATP production or suffering from a neurodegenerative disease. Neurons become inefficient because of poor mitochondrial function due to lowered ATP production. This poor ATP recycling is the signal to the neurons and the brain mitochondria that they are trying to increase proton superconduction in CSF (density of CSF water) to improve CMRO2 and the current in the brainâ€™s other semiconductor, water. The higher CMRO2 is, the less the brain relies on ATP and the more it uses water superconduction. Lactate is like a high octane fuel for neurons.

The lactate supply in the brain is made by up regulation of MCT1 which is called mono carboxylic acid transporter 1.

Ketones can replace much of the human brainâ€™ s requirement for glucose, but they cannot meet the brain's entire energy needs (Cahill, 2006). In addition to providing the carbon to replace oxaloacetate in the tricarboxylic acid cycle, glucose is essential for the brain as the precursor to lactate , which is exchanged when ketones are taken up by the brainâ€™ s monocarboxylic acid transporter.

Ketone utilization is a constitutive feature of brain function because the amounts and activities of the relevant enzymes are not changed by starvation and always exceed the amount necessary to supply the brainâ€™ s energy needs. Hence, the brain is always ready to burn ketones as soon as they are available; a situation consistent with their function as its main backup fuel. The factor that limits ketone use by the brain is therefore the rate of ketone production by the liver and not the brainâ€™s ability to use them. Ketone availability is principally dictated by fatty acid release from fat stores, which in turn is determined by how much insulin levels fall in response to decreasing plasma glucose; it is low insulin that permits the mobilization of fatty acids from fat stores and their conversion to ketones by the liver.........

Ketones come from fat and no where else. This is why humans are designed to have subcutaneous fat and other primates do not.

People with T2D, ME/CFS, AD, PD generally generate a lot of superoxide in the brain as the cause of their disease because they eat too much sugar; Doing so does not allow them to make a lot of ATP (38 vs 147) and not being able to make a lot of ATP and this is why they eat way to many carbs to fast recycle ATP. They can not make ketones well because high insulin levels block ketone production in the liver. When this happens..........superoxide rises..........a lot.

Since superoxide ions and transition metals act in a synergistic manner in the creation of free radical damage, iron supplementation must not be done in patients with any active infections or in general any diseases. Very few people know, much less talk about it. And when iron is liberated in tissues it happens when they lose electrons. This means iron exacerbates all neolithic diseases. This is why so many women have low iron stores.

Neurons in the brain try to avoid superoxide generation at all costs and neurons usually attempt to increase lactate production as a consequence to offset superoxide generation to use lactate as a secondary fuel source to ketones. CSF lactate is a sign.......and this signal is sent through the CSF and into the circumventricular organs. This is where the leptin receptor senses........things from our environment.

Yes, I guess "Insulin resistance" as a reason for obesity is a bit simplistic. We really have to switch back to our original source of nutrients; start using our primary sources of power, instead of the backup to the backup.

In datacenter terms, the primary feed has failed, so we relied on the redundant power source. That failed, so we went onto backup generator. That ran out of diesel, so it's UPS battery backup now. The government is insisting that the manufacturers send more batteries pronto!

### AnnieW — 2013-08-01T22:50:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-question-about-body-shape.7779/post-91333>

Hi friends,

This is mostly a question for the ladies but gents feel free to chime in too. I was wondering if any of you have experienced your body shape changing after doing the leptin reset. I don't necessarily mean weight loss. For example, I have an apple shaped body courtesy of PCOS and insulin resistance. I am all belly and I have zero butt and boobs. I was wondering if following Jack's protocol would help my body even out if that makes sense. It's not that I want a huge butt or expect miracles without plastic surgery or extreme workouts, but it would be nice if things started to look more proportional. I wasn't sure if or how doing the reset would affect fat distribution since it seems to be tied to hormones. It would be nice to change from an apple into a more feminine pear, but could the leptin reset help with that? Am I asking too much?

### caroline — 2013-08-02T00:41:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-question-about-body-shape.7779/post-91338>

Hi Annie! I am an apple shape too. After doing the reset for about 3-4 months I went down two sizes although my weight was up about 2 Kilos. In my opinion the apple shape is driven by hormone issues. It was last August that a young woman in a store asked me why I buy my clothes so big! When you are an apple shape - everything is always too tight and uncomfortable ....at least that it how it was for me. I always longed to be a pear shape too! At least it is more feminine. I didn't seem to notice tho that my clothes were falling off me! Very strange - I guess that I was focused on everything else like food and C/T and trying desperately to understand all the science.

So, now, here we are a year later...my weight is down around 8 kilos and I am down 3 to 4 sizes. I actually have found my rib cage and ribs and my waist! This is the least I can ever remember weighing by quite a bit and I look very healthy ...and feel great.

I was always a yoyo dieter ....as soon as I would lose the weight it would come back on no matter how hard I tried and I would always look gaunt and drawn.

I have noticed lately - my friends don't even mention weight loss . I was talking to a friend the other day and all of a sudden she stopped and said - you look great.
.
So - yes - your body will reshape but it happens bit by bit and sometimes you don't really notice....it is cumulative. Don't make the same mistake I did.....I bought new clothes last September /october and now they are way too big.

For the last month I have been doing kettle bell swings and it is making a big difference in my core strength and my shape and I love it. If I was younger I would definitely do a movnat with Erwan Lecorre!!

It will happen - no question ..... As long as you are 100% committed to dr. K's protocols. Are you epi paleo? I did much better when I added more fat in.

However - don't forget ......food is only 10% ......following light cycles and elimating EMF exposure and Get sunlight and grounding as much as possible - these are way, way more important. Don't under estimate that.

Good luck on this crazy and amazing journey to optimum

# Thread: Everybody just chill out.

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2013-08-03T17:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybody-just-chill-out.7795/post-91430>

See, I knew violence is a preventable disease. Chill.
[http://www.sciencemag.org/content/e...ract?sid=59cd8fac-3931-445d-86e7-855855c577da](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/early/2013/07/31/science.1235367.abstract?sid=59cd8fac-3931-445d-86e7-855855c577da)

### Shijin13 — 2013-08-03T18:05:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybody-just-chill-out.7795/post-91431>

Dali

you make me smile.... I love my cold... wishing for some right now...

### Inger — 2013-08-03T20:35:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybody-just-chill-out.7795/post-91437>

Interesting. I have been thinking about this. Why people seem more violent in warmer countries... I always felt so thankful for living so far north. Here people live with doors open.. day and night, no one cares. Of course, not in the city, but on the countryside this is very common. I always thought it was so cool to live like this! I actually hate locked doors. To not need to be afraid, not at home, not when you walk alone in the woods.. to me this is like paradise for sure. No dangerous animals either! Just freedom..!  Except of one snake, maybe. But easily avoidable.

And what is better than to have another person skin to skin to warm on when it gets chilly... huh! The very best heater I can think of...  pretty useful when it gets a little too cold at times
Maybe that is what makes it more anti violent.. because we need each other for the warming effect, who cares to fight

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-08-03T21:48:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybody-just-chill-out.7795/post-91443>

This is great, just what I needed to see. I made a recent thread about tDCS to basically stop some symptoms I've been experiencing for a long time (psychological/emotional) and they've been randomly coming back (probably due to stress of moving again/brother's surgery/etc.), but yesterday was terrible. I got extremely anxious in Aldi while looking for some dark chocolate and fish, and everything just got weird. I was on the verge of having a panic attack - a bad one; thoughts of this mother whom I knew who killed her youngest son and the girl she was baby sitting flooded my mind - just a lot of negativity. My mind kept wandering over the question as to why _some_ people seem to snap while others can deal with high amounts of stress for their whole lives (such as my mother).... An epsom salt bath followed by a **cold** **bath** afterwards calmed my nerves, yet I woke up in an odd state of mind again today until I swallowed down a bunch of sea salt and took a **cold shower**...

That was a long way of saying that this was perfect timing to see that cold can repair/enhance one's psyche so to speak. It makes a lot of sense now too because I was more or less a 'winter baby' (well, born in November but during a snow storm) and loved the cold all my life. I'm also from a north/eastern European descent where at the minimal there are very cold winters. I think I need the cold, I should live somewhere cold, lol. I can't wait for winter now.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-04T01:07:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybody-just-chill-out.7795/post-91453>

This is just another reason why I get pissed when I hear somebody tell me or tell others cold is just hormetic.......if you do not know what you're talking about just remain quiet.

# Thread: What's Causing This to Happen and How Do I Stop It?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-causing-this-to-happen-and-how-do-i-stop-it.7799/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2013-08-04T14:38:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-causing-this-to-happen-and-how-do-i-stop-it.7799/post-91479>

I need some help determining what is causing the itching.

A few weeks ago, I upped my iodine from 25 mg/day to 50 mg/day and then 75 mg/day. I experienced itching, although not too bad.

Then I had a urinary problem and doctor started me on flomax. One of the side effects of flomax is itching. About the same time I started flomax, I stopped taking one benedryl at night to help with sleep. Benedryl and other antihistamines are often listed as aggravating to bph and I thought it might have been a cause of my urinary problem.

So now my itching is severe and I noticed a few hives.

So what is causing the itching?

-   Detox from iodine
-   Stopping benedryl
-   Flomax
-   Combination of 2 or more of the above

I took a benedryl this morning to see if it helps. Last night, I applied some benedryl lotion I had for poison ivy and it gave me some relief, which is why I took the benedryl this morning.

I believe flomax has an accumulative effect, so I don't think stopping flomax for 1 day would have much of an effect. I could be wrong about this, but many guys who have reported itching, say it started after a few weeks of taking flomax, not on day 1 or 2.

So how do I do my n=1 study? How do I control for 3 variables, both separately and in combination? Or do I just tell the doc I am allergic to flomax and ask him to prescribe something else?

I've tried ct with epsom salts and baking soda. I get relief when I'm in the tub, but itching starts soon after getting out.

ETA: Other side effects of flomax I am experiencing are light headedness (it is an alpha blocker) and fatigue.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-04T16:10:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-causing-this-to-happen-and-how-do-i-stop-it.7799/post-91489>

itching like tinnitus is a sign of energy depletion along sensory microtubules........it means cellular signaling is off for some reason.

### Huck — 2013-08-04T16:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-causing-this-to-happen-and-how-do-i-stop-it.7799/post-91491>

>
> itching like tinnitus is a sign of energy depletion along sensory microtubules........it means cellular signaling is off for some reason.

Any suggestions on what labs might help figure it out?

The benedryl has greatly reduced, but not eliminated the itching.

I'm at a weight I haven't seen in about 30 years, so I imagine my body is dumping a lot of toxins into the blood stream.

### nonchalant — 2013-08-04T18:57:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-causing-this-to-happen-and-how-do-i-stop-it.7799/post-91499>

I think what I would do, based on my experience, is to stop taking the iodine, for a while. I think you are detoxing, yes.
However, I would switch to painting on the iodine, so you still get the benefit, but the effect is localized. Do you have a Magnetico?

I had to stop taking Lugols for a month, to let things settle down and get my selenium caught up. No, I didn't have itching. The max I was taking was 50mg of Lugols, but that was only for a few weeks before I had to stop. I have recently started taking it again, 25mg.

I had awful itching at night at one point in my life when I was drinking lots of diet pepsi sweetened with aspartame. Then there was another point where it felt like swarms of tiny gnats were biting me in bed. That was about the time a cell phone tower was built about 1/2 mile from my home. There is a bank at the base of the cell tower, and sometimes I wonder about the health of the employees.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-causing-this-to-happen-and-how-do-i-stop-it.7799/post-91514>

It sounds similar to a situation I experienced a couple of years ago. I was building to do some high dosing on the iodine and all of a sudden I broke out in a rash on my back. It itched like crazy and yet was slightly raw and had hives all over it. I ended up stopping everything I was taking, except thyroid meds, and it slowly went away. However that took almost 5 months for the itching to leave. My suggestion and I wish I had known this then is to do a salt flush or some kind of flush to get the toxins out quickly. I'm not sure of the exact measurements but 1/2-1 tsp with approx 24-30 oz of good water drank down all at once and it should help. I've since done that with a bromide flush from iodine at a different time and within two days and two doses the rash had subsided considerably and the itching was almost gone.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-91539>

Just finished listening to the July 2013 Webinar, and I'm pretty convinced that a magnetic sleep pad is something to get right away.

Trouble is that I'm all the way down under in Melbourne, Australia. So getting a magnetico pad is out of the question.

However, there's a local supplier who sells magnetic sleep pads which seem to be decently well made. A sample mattress can be seen here -- [http://www.magneticexperience.com.au/king-single-magnetic-underlay-australia-free-postage-p-117.html](http://www.magneticexperience.com.au/king-single-magnetic-underlay-australia-free-postage-p-117.html).

In sum, the King Single sized mattress that I linked to:

\- costs AU$210
\- has 250 magnets, which are all north facing (negative magnetic field)
\- where each magnet has a 1200 surface gauss

It's designed to be used on top of your mattress, but as the magnetico site explains, this could lead to exposure to alternating positive and negative fields.

The magnetico apparently uses 3950 core gauss magnets, which should correspond to a 1000-1100 surface gauss (I have no idea about the calculation, but those are the numbers that seem to pop up on the internet). Magnetico then claims that a single layer of such magnets deliver a 10 gauss magnetic field to the sleeper atop an 8-inch mattress. (the super sleep system relies on 2 layers)

Wouldn't that imply then, that if I place my Australian Magnetic mattress below a standard thickness mattress (8-10 inches), then I would get roughly the same effect as the magnetico? Wouldn't I also be able to stack up 2 of these mattress to achieve the 20 gauss magnetic field?

Thanks.

### _J_ — 2019-02-25T04:37:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-263918>

Something that might interest you..

I used the magnetico website and got some simple quotes + delivery for Australia

If you buy 1x Queen & 2x single, then it is cheaper to have it delivered in the following configuration: -
1x Queen (delivery #1)
2x Single (delivery #2)

You may not think much, but it was about $1000 cheaper. I guess its related to the way that UPS has to handle extremely large/weighted packages, or rather don't want to.

Perhaps UPS have been following the forums, and decided in a non-linear pricing structure ?

# Thread: OK, so you heard the July 2013 webinar, now this..............
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-05T15:02:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91550>

[http://thetrendingmachine.com/poll-shows-18-34-year-olds-are-more-forgetful-than-seniors/](http://thetrendingmachine.com/poll-shows-18-34-year-olds-are-more-forgetful-than-seniors/)

Two-fifths (39%) of Americans have forgotten or misplaced at least one of these everyday items in the past week. Usually we refer to these memory lapses as â€œsenior momentsâ€￾, but The Trending Machine National Poll tells a different story. Millennials 18-34, in fact, are significantly more likely than seniors ages 55 or older to forget what day it is (15% vs. 7%), where they put their keys (14% vs. 8%), forget to bring their lunch (9% vs. 3%) or even to take a bath or shower (6% vs. 2%).

Conversely, the only thing seniors 55+ are significantly more likely to forget are names (23% vs. 16%).

We asked Patricia Gutentag a leading family and occupational therapist what may be at the root of these relatively high levels of forgetfulness in millennials. â€œStress! Stress often leads to forgetfulness, depression and poor judgment.â€￾ Indeed studying for finals and preparing for job interviews, not to mention relationship issues are leading stressors in young adults. Gutentag quickly adds, â€œWe find higher rates of ADHD diagnoses in young adults. This is a population that has grown up multitasking using technology, often compounded by lack of sleep, all of which results in high levels of forgetfulness.â€￾

With regard to gender, the stress associated with work life balance and increased financial responsibilities has taken a toll on women. According to The Trending Machine Poll of 800 adults, women are generally more likely to forget or misplace these everyday items compared to men (43% vs. 31%). Specifically, women are more likely to forget or misplace their keys (14% vs. 8%), their phone (12% vs. 7%), and even to take a bath or shower (5% vs. 3%).

Regional stress differences can be inferred from the higher level of forgetfulness found in the Northeast compared to those reported in the laid back West (51% vs. 39% respectively).

### nonchalant — 2013-08-05T16:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91553>

We need another word for stress. Everyone always thinks of just emotion, pressure, busyness when the word 'stress' is used. Few people think about poor food, lack of energy, hostile environment.

### Martin — 2013-08-05T16:55:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91554>

Amen, nonchalant.

I've had this come up in discussions with my group of friends all the time. They think that just as one can "create" a parking space downtown with just their intention, they can go live in a beach house in Fukashima and get along with just their "thoughts" protecting them. "Stress" to them is ALL mental/spiritual, and imply that all we need to live well is "happy thoughts". And I agree... to a point, but when some disease is still kicking your ass, then one has to look at the environment one is in. And that point is demonstrated by canceling my time-share condo in Fukashima.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-06T00:32:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91583>

Stress in my vernacular is different than everyone else's and has been for a while.......go back and read the first few leptin blogs........when we are LR we have rises in NF kappa beta and IL-6.........anything that causes it.......is what I call stress.

Your field.......needs to lower your IL-6 and NF kappa beta, by the way.........EMF raises both.

Hi guys,

I have been recently consuming 85% dark chocolate to try and treat constipation issues. This successfully brought me up to roughly 1 BM per day however I have noticed a recent significant increase in pounding headaches. I assume this is from leaky gut and blood brain barrier allowing something in dark chocolate through.

I have tried a LOT of constipation remedies and this has been the only one the has brought well formed regular stools. Is there any way to replicate this effect of dark chocolate via other foods/supplements. I have tried various forms of magnesium but they usually stop working after a while and produce kind of messy stools.

Thanks, Sean.

for some, chocolate can trigger headaches - its one of the No-Nos for migraine suffers. But If you follow some of what Dave Asprey talks about - the head aches could be from the mycotoxins in the curing process...

>
> The team predicted that this unprecedented circumstance would lead to the Sun adopting a quadrupole configuration, with both the north and south poles having the same polarity.
> according to observations by â€œHinode,â€￾ it is assumed that the solar magnetic field will be a quadrupole structure, where both the north and south have positive polarity.

> These research results show that the solar dynamo, which is a process generating magnetic fields within the Sun, is bringing about changes seen for the first time since the start of modern-style solar observation. It is believed that the Sun has previously experienced these circumstances during the Maunder Minimum and Dalton Minimum, which are said to have been periods when the Earthâ€™s climate was colder.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-06T23:22:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suns-magnetic-field-how-will-this-affect-us.7811/post-91666>

We are all products of our environment. Our genes are the first draft of what we are created to be, but the experience those genes face (epigenetics) is how the novel of our life is really written. But then we have Maxwell's problems.........While Maxwell's equations (along with the rest of classical electromagnetism) are extraordinarily successful at explaining and predicting a variety of phenomena, they are not exact laws of the universe, but merely approximations. In some special situations, they can be noticeably inaccurate. Examples include extremely strong fields (see Euler–Heisenberg Lagrangian) and extremely short distances (see vacuum polarization). Moreover, various phenomena occur in the world even though Maxwell's equations predicts them to be impossible, such as "nonclassical light" (which really happens) and quantum entanglement (also proven by modern day physics) of electromagnetic fields themselves (quantum optics).

Finally, any phenomenon involving individual photons, such as the **photoelectric effect**, Planck's law, the Duane–Hunt law, single-photon light detectors, etc., would be difficult or impossible to explain if Maxwell's equations were exactly true, as Maxwell's equations do not involve photons. For the most accurate predictions in all situations, Maxwell's equations have been superseded by quantum electrodynamics. ### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-07T00:35:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/example-of-the-field-effect.7818/post-91670>

Who Has the Guts for Gluten?
By MOISES VELASQUEZ-MANOFF

WE know that the proteins called gluten, found in wheat and other grains, provoke celiac disease. And we know how to treat the illness: a gluten-free diet. But the rapidly increasing prevalence of celiac disease, which has quadrupled in the United States in just 50 years, is still mystifying.

Scientists are pursuing some intriguing possibilities. One is that breast-feeding may protect against the disease. Another is that we have neglected the teeming ecosystem of microbes in the gut — bacteria that may determine whether the immune system treats gluten as food or as a deadly invader.

Celiac disease is generally considered an autoimmune disorder. The name celiac derives from the Greek word for “hollow,” as in bowels. Gluten proteins in wheat, barley and rye prompt the body to turn on itself and attack the small intestine. Complications range from diarrhea and anemia to osteoporosis and, in extreme cases, lymphoma. Some important exceptions notwithstanding, the prevalence of celiac disease is estimated to range between 0.6 and 1 percent of the world’s population.

Nearly everyone with celiac disease has one of two versions of a cellular receptor called the human leukocyte antigen, or H.L.A. These receptors, the thinking goes, naturally increase carriers’ immune response to gluten.

This detailed understanding makes celiac disease unique among autoimmune disorders. Two factors — one a protein, another genetic — are clearly defined; and in most cases, eliminating gluten from the patient’s diet turns off the disease.

Yet the more scientists study celiac disease, the more some crucial component appears in need of identification. Roughly 30 percent of people with European ancestry carry predisposing genes, for example. Yet more than 95 percent of the carriers tolerate gluten just fine. So while these genes (plus gluten) are necessary to produce the disease, they’re evidently insufficient to cause it.

Animal studies have reinforced that impression. In mice engineered to express those H.L.A.’s, tolerance to gluten must be deliberately “broken.” Without an immunological trigger of some kind, the rodents happily tolerate the protein.

A recent study, which analyzed blood serum from more than 3,500 Americans who were followed since 1974, suggested that such a trigger could strike adults at any time. By 1989, the prevalence of celiac disease in this cohort had doubled.

“You’re talking about an autoimmune disease in which we thought we had all the dots connected,” says Alessio Fasano, head of the Center for Celiac Research and Treatment at the Massachusetts General Hospital for Children in Boston, and the senior author of the study. “Then we start to accumulate evidence that there was something else.”

Identifying that “something else” has gained some urgency. In the United States, improved diagnosis doesn’t seem to explain the rising prevalence. Scientists use the presence of certain self-directed antibodies to predict celiac disease. They have analyzed serum stored since the mid-20th century and compared it to serum from Americans today. Today’s serum is more than four times as likely to carry those antibodies.

BLAME for the increase of celiac disease sometimes falls on gluten-rich, modern wheat varietals; increased consumption of wheat, and the ubiquity of gluten in processed foods.

Yet the epidemiology of celiac disease doesn’t always support this idea. One comparative study involving some 5,500 subjects yielded a prevalence of roughly one in 100 among Finnish children, but using the same diagnostic methods, just one in 500 among their Russian counterparts.

Differing wheat consumption patterns can’t explain this disparity. If anything, Russians consume more wheat than Finns, and of similar varieties.

Neither can genetics. Although now bisected by the Finno-Russian border, Karelia, as the study region is known, was historically a single province. The two study populations are culturally, linguistically and genetically related. The predisposing gene variants are similarly prevalent in both groups.

Maybe more telling, this disparity holds for other autoimmune and allergic diseases. Finland ranks first in the world for Type 1 autoimmune diabetes. But among Russian Karelians, the disease is nearly six times less frequent. Antibodies indicative of autoimmune thyroiditis are also less prevalent, and the risk of developing allergies, as gauged by skin-prick tests, is one-fourth as common.

What’s the Russians’ secret?

“It’s a remote territory of Russia,” says Heikki Hyoty, a scientist at the University of Tampere in Finland. “They live like Finns 50 years ago.”

At the time of this research, roughly a decade ago, Russia’s per-capita income was one-fifteenth of Finland’s. Analysis of house dust and potable water suggests that the Russian Karelians encountered a greater variety and quantity of microbes, including many that were absent in Finland.

Not surprisingly, they also suffered from more fecal-oral infections. For example, three of four Russian Karelian children harbored Helicobacter pylori, a corkscrew-shaped bacterium, while just one in 20 Finnish children did. The bacterium can cause ulcers and stomach cancer, but mounting evidence suggests that it may also protect against asthma.

Professor Hyoty suspects that Russian Karelians’ microbial wealth protects them from autoimmune and allergic diseases by, essentially, strengthening the arm of the immune system that guards against such illnesses.

Meanwhile, Yolanda Sanz, a researcher at the Institute of Agrochemistry and Food Technology in Valencia, Spain, makes a compelling case for the importance of intestinal microbes.

Years ago, Dr. Sanz noted that a group of bacteria native to the intestine known as bifidobacteria were relatively depleted in children with celiac disease compared with healthy controls. Other microbes, including native E. coli strains, were overly abundant and oddly virulent.

How to determine cause or consequence?

In a test tube, she found that those E. coli amplified the inflammatory response of human intestinal cells to gluten. But bifidobacteria switched the response from inflammation to tolerance.

In rats, the E. coli again intensified inflammation to gluten, prompting what’s sometimes called a “leaky gut” — the milieu suspected of contributing to celiac disease. Conversely, bifidobacteria protected the intestinal barrier. Microbes, it seemed, could influence the immune response to gluten.

Bifidobacteria occur naturally in breast milk, which, along with protective antibodies and immune-signaling proteins, conveys hundreds of prebiotic sugars. These sugars selectively feed certain microbes in the infant gut, particularly bifidobacteria. Breast-fed infants tend to harbor more bifidobacteria than formula-fed ones.

All of which may explain a curious historical phenomenon — an “epidemic” of celiac disease that struck Sweden some 30 years ago. Anneli Ivarsson, a pediatrician at Umea University, recalled a sudden wave of “terribly sick” infants.

Sleuthing revealed that, just before the spike, official guidelines on infant feeding had changed. In an effort to prevent celiac disease, paradoxically, parents were instructed to delay the introduction of gluten until their babies were six months old. That also happened to be when many Swedish mothers weaned their children. Coincidentally, companies had increased the amount of gluten in baby food.

This confluence produced an unwitting “experiment with a whole population,” says Dr. Ivarsson — a large quantity of gluten introduced suddenly after weaning. Among Swedes born between 1984 and 1996, the prevalence of celiac disease tripled to 3 percent. The epidemic ebbed only when authorities again revised infant-feeding guidelines: keep breast-feeding, they urged, while simultaneously introducing small amounts of gluten. Food manufacturers also reduced the gluten content of infant foodstuffs. Dr. Ivarsson found that, during the epidemic, the longer children breast-fed after their first exposure to gluten, the more protected they were.

Not all subsequent studies have found nursing protective, but partly as a result of Sweden’s experience, the American Academy of Pediatrics now recommends that infants start consuming gluten while still breast-feeding.

Research by Dr. Sanz of Spain again illuminates how this may work. Some years back, she began following a cohort of 164 newborns with celiac disease in the immediate family. By four months, children with celiac-associated genotypes — 117 of them — had accrued a microbial community with fewer bifidobacteria compared to those without. If bifidobacteria help us tolerate gluten, these children appeared to be edging toward intolerance.

There was one notable exception: Breast-feeding “normalized” the microbes of at-risk children somewhat, boosting bifidobacterial counts.

Dr. Fasano of Boston has made another potentially important find. He followed 47 at-risk newborns, regularly collecting microbes from 16 of them, which he analyzed after two years. Like Dr. Sanz, he found these genetically at-risk children to accumulate a relatively impoverished, unstable microbial community.

But it’s a secondary observation that has Dr. Fasano particularly excited. Two of these children developed autoimmune disease: one celiac disease, another Type 1 diabetes, which shares genetic susceptibility with celiac disease. In both cases, a decline of lactobacilli preceded disease onset.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-07T00:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/example-of-the-field-effect.7818/post-91671>

Assuming that the pattern holds in larger studies, “imagine what would be the unbelievable consequences of this finding,” he says. “Keep the lactobacilli high enough in the guts of these kids, and you prevent autoimmunity.”

The caveats here are numerous: the tiny sample size in Dr. Fasano’s study; Dr. Sanz hasn’t yet revealed who actually developed celiac disease in her cohort; and even if these microbial shifts reliably precede disease onset — as they do in larger studies on allergic disease — they’re still bedeviled by the old “chicken or the egg” question: Which comes first, the aberrant microbial community, or the aberrant immune response?

Bana Jabri, director of research at the University of Chicago Celiac Disease Center, notes that immune disturbances change the microbial ecosystem. But here’s the catch: Even if the chicken comes first, she says, the egg can contribute. Rodent experiments show that intestinal inflammation can select for unfriendly bacteria that further inflame. “You can have a positive feedback loop,” she says.

SO your microbes change you, but your genes also shape your microbes — as do environment, breast milk, diet and antibiotics, among many other factors.

Such complexity both confounds notions of one-way causality and suggests different paths to the same disease. “You have the same endpoint,” Dr. Jabri says, “but how you get there may be variable.”

The intricacies don’t stop there.

Not all breast milk is the same. It varies according to diet and other factors. One study found that milk from overweight mothers had fewer of those bifidobacteria than milk from thinner mothers. Another observed that breast milk from farming mothers, who inhabit a microbially enriched environment, carried more anti-inflammatory proteins compared with urban mothers’ milk. “All these things are going to matter,” Dr. Jabri says. And they’re all potential nudge points in the quest to prevent disease.

The tangled web of possibilities should not, however, distract us from the facts on the ground. In a far-flung corner of Europe, people develop celiac disease and other autoimmune diseases as infrequently as Americans and Finns did a half-century ago. The same genes exposed to the same quantity of gluten do not, in that environment, produce the same frequency of disease.

“We could probably prevent celiac disease if we just give the same environment to the Finnish children as they have in Karelia,” says Dr. Hyoty. “But there’s no way to do it now, except to move the babies there.”

Moises Velasquez-Manoff is the author of “An Epidemic of Absence: A New Way of Understanding Allergies and Autoimmune Diseases.”

# Thread: My sleep leptin and adenosine links showing up:  Mainstream
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-08T03:30:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/post-91784>

[http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2013/08/06/how-sleep-loss-adds-to-weight-gain/](http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2013/08/06/how-sleep-loss-adds-to-weight-gain/)

Losing sleep tends to make people eat more and gain weight, and now a new study suggests that one reason may be the impact that sleep deprivation has on the brain. This is linked to orexin biology in the retina.

The research showed that depriving people of sleep for one night created pronounced changes in the way their brains responded to high-calorie junk foods. On days when the subjects had not had proper sleep, fattening foods like potato chips and sweets stimulated stronger responses in a part of the brain that helps govern the motivation to eat. But at the same time, the subjects experienced a sharp reduction in activity in the frontal cortex, a higher-level part of the brain where consequences are weighed and rational decisions are made.

The findings suggested that one unfortunate result of sleep loss is this â€œdouble hitâ€￾ in brain activity, said Matthew P. Walker, an author of the study and a professor of psychology and neuroscience at the University of California, Berkeley. A sleepy brain appears to not only respond more strongly to junk food but also has less ability to reign that impulse in.

Some experts have theorized that in a sleep-deprived state, people eat more food simply to make up for all the calories they expend as they burn the midnight oil. But the new study showed that the changes in brain activity were evident even when the subjects were fed extra food and not experiencing any increased sensations of hunger.

Their hunger was no different when they were sleep-deprived and when they had a normal night of sleep, Dr. Walker said. That's important because it suggests that the changes we're seeing are caused by sleep deprivation itself, rather than simply being perhaps more metabolically impaired when you're sleep deprived. This is due to a defect in the adenosine signal due to a loss of orexin neuron loss.

The relationship between sleep loss and weight gain is a strong one, borne out in a variety of studies over the years. Large population studies show that both adults and children are more likely to be overweight and obese the less they sleep at night. In smaller, controlled studies, scientists find that when people are allowed to sleep eight hours one night and then half that amount on another, they end up eating more on the days when they've had less sleep. One pivotal study at the University of Colorado in March showed that losing just a few hours of sleep a few nights in a row caused people to pack on an average of about two pounds.

Other studies have found that the underlying effects of sleep deprivation on the body can in many ways be pronounced. The stress hormone cortisol climbs and markers of inflammation rise. Hormones that stimulate appetite increase, while hormones that blunt it drop. People become less sensitive to insulin, raising their risk of Type 2 diabetes.

But until now, few if any studies have looked at precisely what goes on in the brain when people are starved of sleep and then faced with food decisions. In the new study, which was published in the journal Nature Communications, Dr. Walker and his colleagues recruited 23 healthy men and women and assigned them to two different regimes, each separated by about a week. On one occasion, the subjects came into the lab and got a normal night of rest roughly eight hours before waking up to a small breakfast of toast and strawberry jam.

The subjects then looked at 80 pictures of a variety of foods and were asked to rate how strongly they wanted them while an imaging machine measured brain activity. The subjects were told that after looking through the pictures, they would receive one of the foods that they rated the highest.

On another occasion, the subjects followed the same routine, but this time, instead of sleeping, they stayed awake through the night. They were also given snacks like apples and peanut butter crackers to offset any extra calories that they burned while staying awake.

The research showed that when the subjects were bleary-eyed and sleep-deprived, they strongly preferred the food choices that were highest in calories, like desserts, chocolate, and potato chips. The sleepier they felt, the more they wanted calorie-rich foods. In fact, the foods they requested when they were sleep-deprived added up to about 600 calories more than the foods that they wanted when they were well-rested.

At the same time, brain scans showed that on the morning after the subject's sleepless night, the heavily caloric foods produced intense activity in an almond-shaped structure called the amygdala, which helps regulate basic emotions as well as our desires for things like food and experiences. That was accompanied by sharply reduced responses in cortical areas of the frontal lobe that regulate decision-making, providing top-down control of the amygdala and other primitive brain structures.

One expert who was not involved in the new study, Dr. Kenneth P. Wright Jr., called the findings exciting and said that they help explain why people make poor dietary choices and eat much more than they need to when fatigued.

There is something that changes in our brain when we are sleepy that irrespective of how much energy we need,â€￾ said Dr. Wright, the director of the sleep and chronobiology lab at the University of Colorado at Boulder. The brain wants more even when the energy need has been fulfilled.

But why would a lack of sleep disrupt the brain's response to food?

Dr. Walker said he suspected that one factor that plays a role is a substance called adenosine, a metabolic byproduct that disrupts neural function and promotes sleepiness as it accumulates in the brain. One of the ways that caffeine stimulates wakefulness is by blocking adenosine. Adenosine is also cleared from the system when we sleep.

Without enough rest, adenosine builds up and may start to degrade communication between networks in the brain, Dr. Walker said. Getting sleep may be the equivalent of rebooting the brain.

â€œI think you have about 16 hours of optimal functioning before the brain needs to go offline and sleep, he said. If you go beyond these 16 hours into the realm of sleep deprivation, then those brain networks start to break down and become dysfunctional.

Dr. Walker said it was increasingly clear from the medical literature that there is not a single tissue in the body that is not beneficially affected by sleep.

It's the single most effective thing people can do every day to reset their brain and body health, he said.

### cinnamon — 2013-08-08T04:27:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/post-91793>

Wow. I'm thinking this might help to explain the behaviour of those in a manic state. My experience is with someone dear to me who visits this state (definitely triggered by light cycles and disrupted circadian rhythm) at least once a year - usually for a couple of months. There are stages to his behaviour. What I notice is that it is very childlike in some ways. In a positive way, he is open to new experiences and sees the value of everything. In a negative way, he loses his ability to make rational decisions that go beyond "I want this". So factoring in "can I afford this", "how does it affect others", etc. all but disappears. Like a child, he also reacts very emotionally. And gets a bit paranoid. It's tough to watch and there is no real point in trying to reason with him until the manic episode is over.

Oh, and he gorges on junk food. Doesn't gain weight though. Maybe because he is very active while in this state...

I think I have some reading and rereading to do here to try to understand this a little better.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-08-08T05:32:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/post-91798>

Dr. Kruse, how can you get around sleep deprivation when it happens, and if possible, how can you sleep less? Dave Asprey has a series of blogs on hacking sleep but most delve into technology.

For instance, in the D-Ribose thread, you stated that one day you took 35g of D-Ribose because you only got 4 hours of sleep. Is that a technique since the D-Ribose allows us to create/replenish ATP quickly? Since I'm starting to study the Buteyko method, many practitioners state that the better you get your CP (control pause) the less you should be able to sleep while retaining your health - they show people who have incredibly high CP's (in the 60's) who can sleep about 4 hours or less.

But then again, maybe that shouldn't be our goal, since you have stated before that sleep is our primordial state.

Large population studies show that both adults and children are more likely to be overweight and obese the less they sleep at night. In smaller, controlled studies, scientists find that when people are allowed to sleep eight hours one night and then half that amount on another, they end up eating more on the days when they've had less sleep. One pivotal study at the University of Colorado in March showed that losing just a few hours of sleep a few nights in a row caused people to pack on an average of about two pounds.
>
> Other studies have found that the underlying effects of sleep deprivation on the body can in many ways be pronounced. The stress hormone cortisol climbs and markers of inflammation rise. Hormones that stimulate appetite increase, while hormones that blunt it drop. People become less sensitive to insulin, raising their risk of Type 2 diabetes.
>
> But until now, few if any studies have looked at precisely what goes on in the brain when people are starved of sleep and then faced with food decisions. In the new study, which was published in the journal Nature Communications, Dr. Walker and his colleagues recruited 23 healthy men and women and assigned them to two different regimes, each separated by about a week. On one occasion, the subjects came into the lab and got a normal night of rest roughly eight hours before waking up to a small breakfast of toast and strawberry jam.
>
> The subjects then looked at 80 pictures of a variety of foods and were asked to rate how strongly they wanted them while an imaging machine measured brain activity. The subjects were told that after looking through the pictures, they would receive one of the foods that they rated the highest.
>
> On another occasion, the subjects followed the same routine, but this time, instead of sleeping, they stayed awake through the night. They were also given snacks like apples and peanut butter crackers to offset any extra calories that they burned while staying awake.
>
> The research showed that when the subjects were bleary-eyed and sleep-deprived, they strongly preferred the food choices that were highest in calories, like desserts, chocolate, and potato chips. The sleepier they felt, the more they wanted calorie-rich foods. In fact, the foods they requested when they were sleep-deprived added up to about 600 calories more than the foods that they wanted when they were well-rested.
>
> At the same time, brain scans showed that on the morning after the subject's sleepless night, the heavily caloric foods produced intense activity in an almond-shaped structure called the amygdala, which helps regulate basic emotions as well as our desires for things like food and experiences. That was accompanied by sharply reduced responses in cortical areas of the frontal lobe that regulate decision-making, providing top-down control of the amygdala and other primitive brain structures.
>
> One expert who was not involved in the new study, Dr. Kenneth P. Wright Jr., called the findings exciting and said that they help explain why people make poor dietary choices and eat much more than they need to when fatigued.
>
> There is something that changes in our brain when we are sleepy that irrespective of how much energy we need,â€￾ said Dr. Wright, the director of the sleep and chronobiology lab at the University of Colorado at Boulder. The brain wants more even when the energy need has been fulfilled.
>
> But why would a lack of sleep disrupt the brain's response to food?
>
> Dr. Walker said he suspected that one factor that plays a role is a substance called adenosine, a metabolic byproduct that disrupts neural function and promotes sleepiness as it accumulates in the brain. One of the ways that caffeine stimulates wakefulness is by blocking adenosine. Adenosine is also cleared from the system when we sleep.
>
> Without enough rest, adenosine builds up and may start to degrade communication between networks in the brain, Dr. Walker said. Getting sleep may be the equivalent of rebooting the brain.
>
> â€œI think you have about 16 hours of optimal functioning before the brain needs to go offline and sleep, he said. If you go beyond these 16 hours into the realm of sleep deprivation, then those brain networks start to break down and become dysfunctional.
>
> Dr. Walker said it was increasingly clear from the medical literature that there is not a single tissue in the body that is not beneficially affected by sleep.
>
> It's the single most effective thing people can do every day to reset their brain and body health, he said.

Orexin - _Getting stimulated_

### AutoMoonMonster — 2013-08-08T05:08:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91794>

Sick with three auto-immune diseases for over 12 years (Alopecia Areata, Celiacs, and Ankylosing Spondy), I've tried everything (I think), everything has been a rabbit trail to disappointment. Paleo for two years, been cheating a bit lately, but strong avoidance of Gluten. Dairy and white rice on occasion. Still yet, even before cheating on Paleo my AS symptoms have been on fire lately! My back pain is so sharp sometimes, I could vomit. Anyone here that believes in Jack's routine, or better yet, has had success with it (EMF Rx)? He answered my wife's questions on FB promptly, which I thought was very kind. I'm seeing the top Naturpathic/ M.D./Homeopathic Physician in the country (Dr. Abram Ber) lots of cash and no results. He is 80 yrs. and knows just about every alternative therapy.......but.......never mentioned this EMF stuff. His big thing is avoidance of GMO/ grains and, of course, homeopathic treatment with detox. How do you start this EMF Boot Camp? Is it not just a money making gimmick? Are the results there? Shouldn't an established Neurosurgeon be making bucket loads of cash and not involved in Paleo forums. I don't say this to be challenging or offensive to Dr. Kruse. It's just that I'm in a funk lately. I feel this auto immune shit is hopeless. So can anyone help me. Please. Gary

### Lahelada — 2013-08-08T13:29:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91816>

Is the answer worth 10 bucks ? If so why don't you just try and sleep under an emergency blanket, the foil type used for reheating the body after a trauma or hypothermia. Supposedly they are a shield against EMF . You will need to be careful to put the insulating side to the outside though. If you sleep better you have an answer . If you don't, use the blanket the other way round and see if that brings effects. Is this a proven method ? No. However if my reasoning is right you should not get cellphone or wifi signal under that blanket and that to me in your shoes would be worth 10 bucks for me. I must add that this is my idea, not a recommendation by DR K . I have no Dr or Ing to my name so you will have to decide if my idea has merit .

If it does not, take a week off in the wilderness away from any maddening crowd, cell towers and see if you have any improvements . If you let us know where you live we might be able to help with EMF reduced/free zones. If it does not make you feel different in the slightest you have still had a nice break which you may have needed in the first place. Don't expect huge changes from this though, just enough to wonder if there is something to that EMF lark

It is a case though of help thyself and I do appreciate that you may be sick of just trying by now. However the only way up is through . Just to let you know where I stand on the subject. When I first came here I thought the EMF lark was just a bit of new age woo woo . (Sorry Dr K) I am reconsidering though as I read myself cleverer.
If you don't like reading go on youtube. At worst you will get to laugh at the expense of people who wear tinfoil hats. At best you will find some things that make you go hmmm and make you dig further. A lot of digging involved here. On this journey you have to be bullheaded and allow for being wrong at the same time and develop the wisdom to know to differentiate as to what is called for when.

Good luck whatever you decide to do.
